name: Frederick Abbott
description: Ask Fritz what happened at the Junior Banquet and he will murmur something about "Doc" Mitchell, clad in pajamas, running through the woods back of Gilman Hall, and closely pursued by something or other. Ask him whether he likes to sit in an armchair in a foot of snow and—to tell the truth, we don't know what he would say. At any rate, our guess wouldn't be published. Fritz has plenty of girls on the old string and nobody ever attempts to be with one of them at his time. Maybe it would be useless to even attempt this, and maybe it wouldn't be worth while, but experience shows that it just doesn't happen. In spite of his large harem, Fritz has found time to crack rocks for Dr. Swartz, so that he will know how when he starts doing it for the State. There is doubt if he will ever be caught, he is such a successful dodger. Fritz has avoided more lacrosse balls in his time than even the immortal Mr. Miller. If the regulations only permitted a 4x6 lacrosse stick, Fritz would take up the sport again. Seriously, though, we predict for Fritz a rich wife and a large family, with nothing to worry about except the price of coin.

name: Afable, Miguel B.
motto: "With every change his features play'd, As aspens show the light and shade." —Scott.
description: It's a wonder Afable recognizes himself when he sees his reflection in the mirror. He certainly is one of those people whose facial contours are so arranged that a very slight change in any one of the features effects a complete alteration of his appearance. He changes them all constantly and with varied intensity, thus producing a very interesting effect. One of our chief sources of amusement has been the sight of Afable coming late into lecture, disguised as an ultra-violet light ray or Drosophila Melanogaster. In addition to this, Afable gives excellent promise of becoming a wicked wielder of the knife if he finishes this year without blowing up a laboratory or so. He made the insurance rate on the Chem. Lab. so high that the trustees raised the tuition twice in as many years. In spite of the danger closely connected with his immediate vicinity, we find it worth while to take the chance in order to profit by his congenial and pleasant nature. No matter what the situation, he is always Affable.

name: Fable
description: His features played, light and shade. —Scott. He sees his reflection in people whose facial contours any one of the features effect. He changes them all, giving a very interesting effect. The sight of Afable coming away or Drosophila Melano-gaster in promise of becoming a star without blowing up a Chem. Lab. so high that ears.

name: Askin, Aaron John
description: Far back (we have never been able to determine accurately just how far), but approximately at the beginning of the 20th century, a pink-cheeked, hybrid, brown-eyed baby goo-gooed its way into this world, and smiled wickedly at his pretty nurse. This affection which he showed toward her won for him the delightful appellation of "Minnie", and that same Minnie belongs to him yet! Min passed through the various stages of babyhood with surprising rapid-ity, becoming more beautiful as the years rolled by; so he entered the period of young manhood with a decided advantage over his less sophisticated associates. Four happy years at City College, an application to Hopkins, admission (!!) and lo! Min is metamorphosed. He loses: his nice wuzzy hair, his long trousers, his boyish innocence, his sizzling energy, his democratic nature. He ac-quires: a bald head and a mustache (illustrating the physical law of conservation of matter), a pair of knickers, an aversion to work, an aristo-cratic bearing. Any day at the Barn you may find him sip-ping his tea, bewailing the fact that students are no longer judged by personality; wishing for wealth in exchange for his beauty, and finally, begging the loan of a cigarette. Our Don Juan is headed for great conquests and victories.

name: Morris Randall Baker
description: Now you want to know who this is, don't you? Isn't he cute? Bake has blond hair, and dimples, too. That twinkle in his eye is a mark of distinction that never wears off—no, not even a brutal verbal attack from Dr. Lee could wipe it out. Morris Baker used to be one of the two "Gold Dust Twins." The other was Baker Clarke, the Inseparable Side-kick. Clarke did all the kicking and left us at mid-year; Baker kept quiet and stuck with us. If you closed your eyes and heard these two converse, you could imagine yourself in a bakery. Each called the other "Bake", and it was Bake, Bake, Bake, and still more Bake. Morris hails from the sticks—out on a farm past Reisterstown. His "garsh-darns" couldn't be imitated by Chic Sales himself. But all this means very little because you ought to see him dance. He'll whirl 'em around—oh, so ni-ice, you know. And when it comes to working, giving help when help is needed and minding his own business at other times, it's really surprising how well this little fellow gets along in this big world. During the blizzard, he walked two miles to get a train to come in and take an examination. Yes, Bake surely has that "do-or-die" spirit that will never fail him.

name: Baker, All
description: Isn't he cute? Bake has in his eye is a mark of even a brutal verbal attack. The other Bake did all the kicking and with us. If you closed your imagine yourself in a bakery, Bake, Bake, and still more past Reisterstown.

name: Barker, Jacob Harry Jr.
description: From the ends of the earth to Hopkins came Jacob Harry Barker, Jr., he with the grey lock adorning his noble brow, and with a past that smacks of fiction. His life, as revealed by his autobiography (that requires 10 pages to list the places he has visited, and so has been omitted), has been crowded with events so stirring as to make Jack London's tales appear like the adventures of Chief Justice Taney on Mt. Vernon Place. Whether he came to our University in quest of further thrills or merely seeking a haven of rest after his past experiences, he hasn't made known, but it is safe to say that had knowledge of his varied accomplishments been more widespread, he would have been invited to appease his taste for adventures by taking a hand at subduing those wild, unleashed music-eaters who constitute one of the last heritages of barbarism yet in evidence on our campus. Jake is a musician, and even now performs on a pipe organ at one of the many large movie houses in the great city of Annapolis. He commutes every day, but, strange to relate, he still retains a most suave disposition and graciousness of manner. With his learning in the fields of Political Science and the Romance Languages he should spread far and wide throughout the lands of his travels the fame of his alma mater.

name: August John Bauernschmidt, Jr.
description: Bismarck, 'tis said, kept silence in seven languages. Sphinx-like Barry is two languages and a dialect ahead of the Count. If silence is golden, Jack is the original Eldorado Kid and the Spirit of 1849 rolled into one. He would have as much chance in a debate with Gallaudet as the average man has with his wife. But with a lacrosse stick in his hand—oh, mummah, say it with flowers. One cannot look at Barry without thinking of lacrosse. For four years an ace on the Varsity close attack, he has proved a giant in the scoring, having been the highest individual point scorer last year. Once he is started, nothing can stop him—that is, except the usual maidenly dialogue that takes place close to the sidelines: “Where is he?” and “Oh, there he is.” Speaking of the tantalizing sex, it is a marvel how they can force him to do the Jekyll-Hyde stunt. It is a well-known fact that a noted song writer met Jack at an affair one evening, and after observing his change from the staid to the frisky, returned to Broadway and wrote the now-famous song, “You'd Be Surprised.” Seriously speaking, though, few outside of his close friends know what a great heart lies beating beneath those thin ribs. To those who know him intimately, Barry is a great pal, one who is always willing to give his hearty support to any suggestion, and who is willing to do anything for a friend.

name: Leroy Bernard Baughman
clubs: Y. M. C. A., 1, 2, and President, 4
description: Hark ye, and hear ye well; for great is this young man's ability upon the piano and trombone. Yet this talent has its drawbacks, for only too often amid the din of study (save the mark!) in the E. E. Senior room will he burst forth into gladsome tunes. However, we don't know anything that Roy can do better than tickle the ivories of a piano, unless it be the breaking of hearts among the gentle sex. Once upon a time we thought him very serious and entirely unconcerned about the ladies, but that was before he spent two years in the 117th Trench Mortar Battery. Now that he has returned, and we have learned to know him better, we are no longer fooled by such expressions as: "Well, I must attend a Y. M. C. A. meeting this hour"; for we know that he probably means to meet one of his er—a—friends at Charles and 31st to take her for a ride on a Blue Bus. But with all his fancies (for it may be truthfully said that he really expects to hear music pour forth every time he throws a switch or pushes a button), Roy is quite a good student, and if his class work is a fair sample of his ability along the engineering line, we know that success will always be his.

name: Harold Cranston Beall
motto: "A book of verses underneath the bough, A jug of wine, a loaf of bread, and thou. —Omar Khayyam."
clubs: J. H. U. Student Chapter A. S. C. E., 3, 4
description: Harold is a member of the younger set of our class, and certainly not an engineer by nature. He clearly belongs in an environment of poetry, painting, and things far removed from hard, matter-of-fact formulae, concrete, steel and other of the engineer's stock in trade. He rather dwells in a world of romances, exotic dreams, and the like. We of his class cannot but observe the fact that he seems to be making wonderful strides along this line; that is, if any weight can be attached to the large number of letters addressed in a feminine hand that he constantly receives. The best of luck to you, Harold. May you shine as an engineer even as your auburn locks shine in the sunlight, and may your life be always as bright as is the sunshine of Virginia—the place where your thoughts have so often turned.

name: Beall, N.
description: Our class, and certainly not an environment of poetry, matter-of-fact formulae, stock in trade. Exotic dreams, and the like. He seems to be making wonderful progress, and that he constantly remains an engineer even as you. May your life be always as bright.

name: Bell, Vernal Augustus
clubs: Acad. (Pol. Econ.) Prince Ed. Is., Canada
description: Vern, as everyone calls him, hails from Prince Edward Island, Canada. At Prince of Wales College, Charlottetown, his prowess with the hockey stick made him a leader in winter sports. Although a keen student of Political Science and Economics, his aspirations lead to things purely agricultural; he has but one ambition—to be the Potato King of his insular Paradise. In seeking the above-mentioned throne, Vern seems destined to be ably assisted. A recent purchase at a jeweler's, frequent calls on a most charming Miss, and kindred actions, surely presage something matrimonial. As Mogul of the Ebaugh-Bell-O'Keefe Triumvirate, his influence was most benign. His joviality and sociable manner, supplemented by a standing bid to everyone to share the spare-tire space in his Chandler Chummy, kindled for him many fires of true friendships. Before coming to Hopkins he had made a promising start at Boston University Law School. He was captain of the hockey team, and a member of Delta Theta Phi. A brief army career seems to have inspired a change of plans, so following his discharge he came here. Unfortunately for Hopkins, duties not unlike those of a consul have occupied Vern's spare time, and precluded his participation in athletics. Here's hoping every spud is a watermelon in size, and that they will be as abundant as the sands of the seashore.

name: Victor Leopold Benning
hometown: Cape Town, U. of S. Africa
clubs: Head of Publicity Department of Hopkins Chapter A. S. M. E., 3, 4
Foreign Secretary to Y. M. C. A., 4
description: When the Engineers heard two years ago that they were to have a classmate from South Africa, discussion ran high, and bets were placed as to whether he would be a dark ebony or a high yellow. Imagine our surprise when, on opening day, in walked Victor, fair of hair, blue of eye, and with a dignified bearing that was the envy of us all. Which of us, we asked ourselves, could face a cold, cruel world in a piccadilly collar and cylindrical trousers with the self-possession that characterized Victor? Vic soon became acclimated, and realized that the modes in Cape Town and in Baltimore were different. He changed his outer garb, but not himself, for there was no need for that. A good scout is a good scout in Hell, Heaven or Hopkins, and needs no naturalization. It took Vic a little while to learn our slang and also that we did not mean all we said, but having learned, he started kidding us as much as we kidded him; with a slight advantage on his side, perhaps. No sketch of him would be complete without mentioning his camera. At every important event (St. John's, Foch, Snow), Vic and his camera were always on the job. The famous M. E. Art Gallery, which was confiscated during Christmas-time, was not his work, however. Well, Vic, here's to you and to her, whoever she may be. We would you were to remain with us, but if South Africa's gain must be our loss, we wish both you and your country luck. Fifty

name: Samuel Bernstein
description: To whom it may concern—here is our human Question Mark. Bernie can ask more questions to the square inch, and deeper ones, too, than Edison. When he asks you for an explanation, you take him slowly through a series of proofs, and he will profess total understanding. Then when you're through, he pops the original question at you again. Isn't that enough to drive you crazy? During a class lecture our young hopeful may be seen with his eyebrows arched, nose uplifted and lips pursed, so that his whole countenance represents a perfect interrogation point; indicating clearly that Bernie is doubtful as to whether or not the prof.'s theories are correct. The queer part of it is that he can understand some deep mathematical discussion "right off the bat," but something which is 100 percent simple will puzzle our hero for days and days. Sometimes, when everyone in the Senior E. E. room is studying (?), Bernie will burst forth rather spasmodically with a few lines of some passionate love song (which according to Hoyle could come from Burnham alone—or maybe Smith), and then he will remain quiet for another week. With all this, however, we like Bernie for his stick-to-it-iveness, and expect great things from him.

name: Berry, Thomas Lansdale
motto: "Many happy days together!"
clubs: R. O. T. C., 1, 2, 3, 4, and Captain, 3, 4
description: TOM BERRY is a fine fellow, and that's saying something. He is a first-rate chap, well liked by all who know him. He is one of the few men in the class who can get all his work in on time without overworking himself, although it is a puzzle to us how he does it. Ah, we have it, it's always easier to work when we have someone to work for, an inspiration, an O. A. O. (one and only). No, Tom, this is no ragging; we merely want to congratulate you on your choice. Tom likes to spend a good part of his time talking to his classmates or fraternity brothers on subjects pertaining to the most irrelevant matters. He is a fairly bright chap, though, and can usually back up all he says. We wonder what kind of glasses Tom must wear that compel him to crawl within a hair's breadth of our nose whenever he wants to tell us something. Keep it distant, Tom, you may eat onions some day and forget yourself. Tom was in France during the war, and can tell you many a yarn about gay Paree. He will stop his work at any time to help a fellow-man in a distressful struggle with some engineering problem. Well, Tom, here's how we'll bid you good-bye:—"Many happy days together!"

name: Blechman, Abraham Joel
description: The HULLABALOO would be incomplete, to be sure, if a representative of the "Homewood contingent" at Pimlico were omitted from the pages of this annual. The task of selecting a delegate from among so great a number of eligibles was, to say the least, arduous, but still we feel that in Blechman we have made a choice that will prove entirely free from objection—Blechman's included. Of all our followers of the sacred ponies, Al is perhaps the most scientific. Scientific by nature (a Pre-med., to be sure), he has acquired a prolific vocabulary of the track, and while the "season" is on, the Homewood ozone is just vibrating with the peculiar waves due to this highly complicated terminology. Two-year-olds, however, are not the only things that hold charms for Blechman. Art courses and Philadelphia both receive his attention; we have yet to solve the mystery that relates these two of his prime interests. Still, the boy is a good sport, in both senses of the word. To show his versatility, he has found time to pass his courses in rather good fashion, and has further developed the rare art of making friends. We know he is sincere; when he enters the Med. School, he will "put away childish things." We have seen enough of his real ability when seriously directed to predict complete success.

name: Benson Bloom
description: The whole blooming truth about Bloom is that he is a darned good fellow, although a little reserved and sedate. Benny, a "pre-med." possessed of the true scientific spirit, delights in experimenting with all sorts of things; for instance, sticking pins in dead cats to see if there is any reflex, etc., etc. Though he shows some indications of being a materialist, he is possessed of an aesthetic soul, as is evidenced by his schedule, replete with Arts courses. For example, he will scrutinize a girl's face not to view her natural external beauty, but rather to admire the sensitive nostrils, receding brow, and all other evidences of temperament and soul beauty. To him, the scientist, all sentimental things as eyes, complexion, limbs and build are bosh; he thinks in terms of optics, epidermis, gastrocnemius and the like. Bloom has made the acquaintance of several female students of the Maryland Institute—long-haired, goggle-eyed, Ruthenian type,—but his friendship is purely Platonic. They do say, however, that one little "PEACH" has succeeded in vamping him, but the symptoms are not yet distinct. Benny is a splendid student and a good athlete, and if he would take some advice and round out his education on the neglected side, his success in his chosen calling would be assured.

name: Bloom
description: He is a darned good fellow. Benny, a "pre-med," possesses interests in experimenting with all sorts of things, like dead cats, to see if there are indications of being a materialist. This is evidenced by his schedule, replete with analyzing a girl's face not to view the sensitive nostrils, but to appreciate temperament and soul beauty. To him, complexion, limbs, and build are just gastrocnemius and the like.

name: Andrew Lyle Bolton
motto: "Proud Nimrod first the bloody chase began, A mighty hunter. . . ." —Pope.
description: During the first part of his college career Lyle majored in two subjects—Golf and Women—and minored in Chemical Engineering. His last year saw a great change in his major subjects. He is particularly famous for his war on "Wows," which (for the benefit of those who have missed the thrill of hearing these famous stories from the hunter directly) we will add the "Wow" is three times the size of an elephant. His uncontrollable temper often caused great fear among his classmates, for when someone would thoughtlessly displease him, he would burst forth with the fierce ejaculation: "Don't get me angry, boy." Lyle has indeed a most extensive vocabulary, composed chiefly of such phrases as "Heck on that", "Am I good?" "Wow!" If he has ever passed a drugstore without suggesting a "Chocolate Beer" it is a sure bet that his finances were somewhat short of 17c. When you begin your profession, may you always be able to buy them by the gross.

name: Brawner, Henry Pierce
clubs: Chairman Sigma Chi Beta Entertainment Committee, 2
description: It is with more or less a lack of confidence that we attempt to elucidate and extol the merits of the widely-known H. P. Brawner, inasmuch as Pierce has had much more practice along these lines himself. At the outbreak of the late unpleasantness, Brawner left St. James' School and enlisted. He soon saw active service in France, where he was gassed. Unfortunately this unpleasant experience has not caused him to have any mercy upon his college mates. Pierce has made a splendid record for himself at college. Whatever he lacks in tact he more than makes up for in efficiency. He is very efficient. Not content with "going through" in three years, he has acted as manager of football, and has taken the leading part in the Dramatic Club productions, in addition to serving as business manager of the club this year. He is now the proud wearer of a well-earned O. D. K. emblem. Pierce's boundless energy, perseverance and efficiency should do much to carry him forward toward the attainment of his ambitions, and we are very glad to learn that he will take up graduate work next year and at the same time manage the football team, for, we hope, an even more successful season. Keep it up, Juanita!

name: Bryant, Charles B.
motto: "Buy Buns."
description: Charlie didn't really intend to come to Hopkins, for he first went to Carnegie Tech. Here he found that his good marks were all being wasted, as there was no chapter of T B Π, so he entered Hopkins in 1920, and soon achieved his ambition along that line. We are certainly glad he decided to bestow himself on us, not only because of his pleasant personality, but also for the enjoyment to be derived from "kidding" him. (Just ask him about the Senior Prom and the part Winnie Harden had in it.) Nuf Ced. It was he who introduced the famous slogan of the Chems: "Buy Buns." This sterling young man has applied himself industriously to study, but has found time to contribute to the success of the Glee Club with his resounding first bass. It is rumored that upon graduation he will teach for a while in a certain college—co-ed, we presume. If this be true, it is to be hoped that he will continue to hold in undying admiration all the ladies who have charge of stock rooms. A blockhead with melodious voice, In boarding schools may have his choice. —Swift. Fifty-seven

name: Burnham, Alfred Huntington Jr.
description: SPATS, muffler, a torn gray suit for work, a cushion that has seen better days, and legs that have gone long ways—all of the 57 into 1—that's Heintz Burnham, even though he does hate necktie salesmen. Heintz is an A No. 1 college man, the kind we all like to have with us. When he is not running all over Homewood Field cleaning up with his lacrosse stick, he is spending his summer vacations overrunning Germany, Italy and Algiers, collecting funny stories of every nationality. He surely has some legs to run with, and he looks like six o'clock alongside of quarter-after-nine Chunky Wells. Then when it's time for funny stories no one is more frivolous and overflowing with glee than is Heintz. The rapidity with which he fires off witty expressions of the craziest kind throws us all into somersaults. Now put to this a bright, hard-working student, who likes his work and expects to make a lot of it, and you have an inkling of the personality of our duke, Heintz Burnham. Yes, Heintz, your classmates surely have learned to like you, and your leaving will be a bitter dose to swallow.

name: Cabe, Melvin Roy
description: Behold the tiniest fellow in the Senior Chem. Class—no, not mentally, but physically. "Bob" (where he ever got hold of this name we don't profess to know) has been with us four years, and has always impressed us with the fact that nothing worries him—with the possible exception of exams, and since they worry even the best of us, we will excuse him for that single failing. Bob works on the three-out-of-five basis, coming to school now and then, and performing an experiment, then picking up Whittington and Navvy and with them visiting all the shows and movies in town. Bob's reports on the character of the shows carry great weight with the rest of the chems. If he praises a show, the next day the rest of the chems will be found in that movie. His influence here is absolute, and his judgment has always been found to be of the best. We would like to have Bob around more frequently, for, with his cheerful never-worry attitude he keeps us in the best of spirits—quite an accomplishment in these days, even for a chem.

name: Caplan, Samuel Harry
description: Speaking of thirst for learning, there are men who are willing to come all the way from China, Africa, and where not, in order to quench it. But our friend Caplan has it over them all; every day, without fail, one can spy on the Ellicott City Limited bound for Homewood, a young man with a serious, scholarly physiognomy, devouring one of those big, fat books which are supposed to give one the fundamentals of a pre-medical training. That young man is Sam H. Caplan, who expects some years hence to hang out on Ellicott City's Main Street a shingle: Sam H. Caplan, M. D. Yes, Caplan aspires to be the Dr. Kendicott of Ellicott City, and if desire for knowledge and willingness to work count for anything, we are sure that he will attain his objective. Interested as he is in one of the leading department stores of the quaint old Maryland town, he has numerous responsibilities other than his college work; he has about realized that the time is soon to come when he must choose definitely between the two—medicine, or business. Though we believe that in either field Caplan would make his mark, we are rather confident that his perseverance will guide him to the calling of his original choice.

name: Caplan
description: He is willing to come in order to quench it. Without fail, one Homewood, a young one of those big, fat is of a pre-medical course some years hence. H. Caplan, M. D. If desire for we are sure that he will succeed.

name: Clarke, Louis Alfred
description: Louis was a more or less unknown quantity in his first year and a half at the University. As a candidate for the baseball team in his second year he was not very successful, attracting attention chiefly by his speed on the bases. This led someone to suggest that he enter the inter-class track meet; merely as a matter of class spirit he donned a track suit and ventured forth. In the very first event, the 100-yard dash, he astounded everyone by defeating the pick of the Varsity. A few minutes later he romped away with the 220, and was immediately signed up by the track coach. This event ended his baseball aspirations. During the next few weeks he entered and won the dash events in several of our dual meets. Then, with only a month's experience, he was entered in the M. A. S. A. A. Championships against some of the best in the country. Here he covered himself with glory by running a close second to Lecong, of Lafayette, in the 100 and 220 yard dashes in the remarkable time of 9.4 and 21.3. This year he gives promise of being even faster than last season, and if so, will become one of the best track stars in the country, with an enviable reputation. Though not showing quite the same speed in his studies, he has given many evidences of adaptability and perseverance that augur well for the future.

name: Morris Cohen
motto: "HOMEOPATHIC CHIROPRAXIS" (two bucks a visit is what I axes). This you'll read upon my shingle, "Push the bell and hear it tingle."
description: Descensus Avernis Facile Est. Ex tribu sacerdotis* sum, hence my taste for good old rum. I was born in a cradle of gold, and nursed on silver hydrasol. Diamonds and pearls upon my toes; eating and sleeping is all I knows. When I says No—then No it be; when I says Yes—you must agree, for I never was known to pick a bone.—I'm no dog, I'm Morris Cohen! In the realms of science shall I wander, a million bucks will I squander. Patent medicines I'll discover. Unknown cures will I uncover. So—I'll seal the blind and halt the lame, I'll send them back from whence they came. Dead or alive, they'll get a good ride; one good shove, and down they'll slide. Oh—the road to fame is easy! As I gaze on the mundane puerility, my concept of transcendental subjectivity forges to the fore. And I says, says I, with a long-drawn sigh, "This ain't no place for I. For I am I, and ergo sum." Quod erat demonstrandum.

name: Cotton, H. Kyd Douglas
motto: "Heb. VI, ii."
description: DOUG is one of our champion non-athletic activities men. The News-Letter was early marked out by this Maniac (Citizen of Maine) to come under his power, and his ambition in this direction was fulfilled when he became Editor-in-chief, in which position he conducted the paper through a remarkably successful growth, and evolved a bi-weekly from what had always been a weekly publication. Doug was also responsible for the Daily Bulletin of last year, and the official Bulletin Boards of this term. He was a founder and the first business manager of our well-known Black and Blue Jay, an officer in O. D. K., and president of the Student Activities Council, for the foundation of which he deserves the lion's share of credit. We might appropriately say that here is a leader—a molder, in fact—of college activities, and as such a student who refuses to cotton to anyone. You can't kyd him. Indeed, those who have doug below the surface know that he has ever been sincerely bent on doing all in his power for Hopkins. With his remarkable facility for getting away with good marks without losing sleep over his lessons, and with his proven executive ability as examples, he should speedily rise to the top in whatever line of endeavor his future career is cast; and such is our prophecy.

name: Davis, Charles Clifford
motto: "Why the fireworks?" asked a citizen of Wilna, Md., one June evening in 1900. "We're on the map, idiot!" replied another. "Cliff just arrived."
description: As years went on after this famous event he grew, and eventually passed into long trousers and the Belair High School. By hard work (incredible) he won a scholarship to the J. H. U. However, the exertion must have been too great; at least he has never shown any signs of overwork since entering college. We wonder why "Dave" always leaves the "lab." early on Friday afternoons. Dame Rumour hath it that he must repair to his domicile in time to doll up before departing to waste the time of some fair one. You never can tell, you know. During the summer vacations Dave can be found working on explosives and poisonous gases at Edgewood Arsenal; his interest in the latter article is only technical, not for practical use. He expects to follow his chosen field of work, chemistry, on graduation, so here's wishing him the best possible luck.

name: Albert Edward Dimond
clubs: Alt. Varsity Debating Team, 1, and Team, 2, 4
description: In the ever-distant future we see a small, Napoleonic creature striding over the prostrate forms of his victims. He is no military hero, nor is he one of these fanciful human-demons beloved of moving pictures and unnecessarily romantic people. His victims are the unhappy lawyers who engaged in combat with him. These and the savants who challenged his philological knowledge and he—well, of course, it’s Albert Dimond. We happen to like Albert very much because he is entirely free from most college prejudices (whether they are Freshman or Senior prejudices), and because he is keen enough not to be so ignorantly boring as are “Many-College-Students-I-Have-Known.” He’s quite human, too, for he was an altogether good candidate for the baseball team, a tennis player and a ready “mixer”—though we assure you that he holds himself aloof from most. Albert has won nearly all the honors that can be won for speaking, declaiming or in general, doing those things that distinguish politicians. He has taken classes leisurely and yet done exceedingly well in studies. He has frightened numerous Engineers and others with people like Villiers de l’Isle-Adam. He has—but never mind. I should really tell you his faults, too. However, we may forget them because he possesses that one gift that is so often denied to numerous footballers and college heroes—a sense of humor.

name: Roy Melvin Dorcus
description: He thinks, with his timid blue eye, to pass for a blossom exceedingly shy. Apologies to F. S. Osgood. He runs like a shot R. O. T. C. (right out the cannon). In fact, it has been a matter of serious conjecture in the Physics Department as to just what becomes of his legs and arms when doing the "220." He makes it look like a case of short measure. Speaking of girls (as we must eventually, so why not now), we come to a dark and impenetrable mystery as far as Roy is concerned. We do know that in one instance, when Roy was dressed to the point of discomfort, he took a running departure from a certain group of gentlemen to go somewhere to see someone. Now that someone has not yet been discovered, and the chances are 100 to 1 that she never will be. If you could only see that twinkling side glance, and delightfully self-conscious smile whenever pumping is attempted. He used to ride a motor-cycle, but she, like many another girl, threw over her "steady" for a better man; that explains his Henry Roadster. Really, we like him quite well, and would better, if he did not keep to himself the pep that we know he possesses.

name: James Wilson Duffy
description: Rumor hath it that Wilson Duffy appeared in this world with a tennis racquet under his arm, and that his balls were even at that early date forceful and calculated to produce the best results. Whether there is any truth or not in this assertion, it is certainly true that Wilson's ability at tennis has stood him in good stead at J. H. U., as may be testified by anyone who has loitered around the courts or happened to play with him in this fine spring weather. Wilson is certainly the cat's hirsute adornment when it comes to tennis; he has been the team for the last few years. When not playing tennis, Wilson's chief purpose in life seems to be the caking of an already overcaked pipe, much to the disgust of a certain overworked janitor. At times he has been known to smile (not that he is of a sour disposition, but merely given to repression), but such times are few and far between, in college hours, at least, and we have a queer suspicion that he saves his smiles for the flappers. We regret this, because we feel sure that by his silence Duff conceals much information which will be valuable to him in after life, and which we would be glad to hear.

name: Ebaugh, Cameron Duncan
description: A dynamic individual with an unusual and distinguished past. First becoming an object of parental admiration in Chambersburg, Pa., he sojourned there until his high school days, when his folks came to Baltimore. Two years of diligent scholarship at City College equipped him for a foreign career of seven years in old Porto Rico. During the early days of the World War, "Dunk," as his intimate friends dub him, was laboring for a commission at an Officers' Training Camp. Three months, and our hero stalks forth with gold bars. After a few weeks of active service he proved himself worthy of more than a shave-tailship, and was commissioned a First Lieutenant. Much to his disappointment, the Armistice came on the eve of his departure for France. Desirous of obtaining a college education, he returned to Baltimore after his discharge, and entered Hopkins and the Class of 1922. His distinguished scholarship while here, three years of art at the Maryland Institute, and a thorough knowledge of Spanish, should adequately assure a successful future in his chosen field. Many of Porto Rico's prominent citizens of tomorrow will reflect the fruits of Duncan's pedagogic efforts in the schools of San Juan.

name: Warrick Rigeley Edwards, Jr.
motto: "Choice word and measured word, above the reach of ordinary men."—Wordsworth.
clubs: Member Team Holding Hopkins Indoor and Outdoor Mile Relay Record
description: RIGELEY (note the spelling) entered Hopkins from Poly with advanced standing, and has never quite overcome this great handicap. On the cinder path, however, he has overcome even greater handicaps, and for the three years of his college residence has been one of the shining lights of the Track Team—a "headlight," as it were. For an engineer—and a Chemical Engineer at that—Warrick (again note the spelling—all rights reserved, including translation into the Scandinavian) is an unusually well-read young man, being perfectly familiar with all the old literary masters and not a few of the new. His elocutionary ability should stand him in good stead; for should the reactions of Chemistry ever become too violent for him, his success in the world of drama would be assured. And yet, forsooth, he is withal a quaint, unassuming chap, who should some day rise to a high place in the world, but whether this place will be in Chemistry or not remains to be seen.

name: Emmart, Adolphus Dunan
description: A. D. EMMART, philosopher, poet and biological authority, was born in Baltimore, Md., in 1903. He received his elementary education in the schools of his native city, matriculated at the Johns Hopkins University in 1919, and took his Ph. D. in biology in 1925. He became instructor in the same University, and later professor. In 1928 he married a millionaire octogenarian, who outlived him by five minutes. He became the father of a large and comely family, numbering eight girls and two boys. His death in 1935 was caused by acute hardening of the epidermis of one of the digits of the pedal extremities. We shall consider Mr. Emmart first as a poet. A protagonist of the Sandburg school, he achieved notable success in that form of expression. The following is an example of his well-known ability: I saw an oyster sitting on the seashore, Near the sea, seeing what the sea saw. See! ? ! In biology, Mr. Emmart made remarkable contributions to science, such as "Observations on the Habitual Instincts of Vertebrate Protozoa." In this magnum opus, he proved that the highest form of animal life is found in the amoeba, and that man is gradually sublimating toward this form. In philosophy, Dr. Emmart subscribed to the school of Dr. Frank Crane. Dr. Emmart was a great man, a faithful husband and a good father.

name: Engel, Gilson Colby
description: BEHOLD the Toastmaster and President of the Class! Gilson first came into prominence at our Junior Banquet, where his eloquence and dry wit endeared him to the hearts of all. From that time his rise has been rapid, and his skill as an elocutionist has been found indispensable by the Interfraternity Board, the Dramatic Club, and the Class. Lately we have observed that he has acquired the ability to accompany his words with gestures appropriate to each occasion—an ability which he possibly derived directly from his services as cheer leader this fall. "Gil's" chief source of pride, and the envy of his classmates is—yes, you guessed correctly (don't feel insulted, because it is guesswork on our part, "Gil")—it can be seen distinctly—with imagination—there on his upper lip. This, he claims, is his passport to Goucher, and as such he makes constant and frequent use thereof. Some day we are going to commandeer it, and with it as a disguise learn the intimate secrets of half the population of our sister college. Although "Gil's" rise to fame is like that of a skyrocket, we feel sure that his finish will not be a fizzle, because his popularity is backed up by many substantial qualities which will insure his success.

name: Fairbank, Charles William
clubs: Secretary Student Chapter A. S. M. E., 3
description: From the war-torn fields of Belgium to the quiet of Homewood Campus came this veteran. But, fierce reader, don't mistake our meaning; "Charlie" doesn't drink, and doesn't even gamble. Apparently his one great failing is the smoking of what he calls cigars, but what we call by several unprintable names. We have always been in favor of passing a law forbidding the manufacture of his particular brand of smokes, but he has never been beguiled into betraying their name. Possibly his family clothesline could a tale unfold. "Charlie" has a keen mind and a brand of repartee that is rather hard to match, as most of our wits have found to their chagrin. Really, we don't know what the Engineering School will do for a timekeeper when he leaves. The schedule will be thrown into chaos and bells will ring at strange and irregular hours. Every afternoon when "Charlie" gets up to wend his homeward way there is a general movement throughout all the class rooms, as the students, janitors, and faculty members reach for their watches to set them at four o'clock. Twice he fooled us, and left at 2:30, with the result that all classes were disarranged until the following afternoon. The peculiar part of it is that he gets as much work done as the rest of us who stay here until six or after. We don't know how he gets that way, but we certainly envy his speed. His cheery disposition has helped us over many a dull day.

name: Feldman, Milton
description: Way out in the wilds of New Jersey, there is, hidden miles away from civilization (i. e., where the ducks go barefoot in their pedestrian activities), a little village. Here our hero was born and bred, under the best influences of the daily sunshine and nightly moonshine. Yea, even to this isolated place spread the renown of Johns Hopkins, and Milton decided to cast his humble lot with us. After—but let us first describe the youth who here frowns down upon the interested reader. He is, of course, as all heroes are, handsome, but bright, nevertheless—one of those three-year prodigies—sincere, good-natured, deliberate, serious. He claims that even in the town from which he hails they play the manly game of football; that he played for his high school team, and that on such and such a day, when their rivals held them to a score of 6-0, until he entered the game and—oh, well, the rest is easy. Our friend has but one serious fault; he has a well-developed habit, much to Dr. Cowles' chagrin, of walking out of the Biology Lab for a “few minutes” (leaving at 1:15 and returning at 3:55). Despite that, we still maintain that “Milt” is a good skate. He intends to study medicine. Lands, here’s hoping he doesn’t forget his patients as he does his laboratories!

name: Fontz, Robert
motto: "Let sleeping 'dogs' lie."
description: Four years ago there appeared on the horizon at Homewood the figure of a country boy, with freckled face and modest air; whose gait showed him more accustomed to bare feet and St. Michaels roads than "Step-over" shoes and city streets. Those who may recall his arrival will never cease to wonder at the truly phenomenal change that four years of college life have worked in "Bob"—for, as you have undoubtedly guessed, it is "Bob" that we have been describing. Just walk down the 2600 block of North Charles Street any sunshiny day, or cloudy day, or any kind of a day, and one sight will particularly impress itself upon your memory—two feet, clad in the niftiest of brogues, reposing on the sill of a well-known house. This feat is his chief accomplishment, just as those feet are his "sole" possessions. Though not possible of recognition, these dogs are the same two that brought "Bob" all the way from St. Mike's. All things are possible through the agency of a college education.

name: Harrison Sales Forgrave
description: "TINY" goes around with a worried expression, as though he is searching for a lost collar button and doesn't know exactly where to look. Perhaps there is a collar button in the case; maybe it is a woman; or, perchance, a tax collector is the cause of it all, but we really suspect "Tiny" of absent-mindedness, concentration, or deep thinking; call it what you will, the result is the same. It is very seldom that he can be induced to talk for any length of time; he seems always to be in a hurry to get back to his thoughts, giving vent to an occasional spontaneous outburst at long intervals, and then immediately lapsing into silence. Curiosity is one of "Tiny's" failings. He wants to know exactly how an amoeba feels when it has stomach ache, so he keeps one poor little cuss under observation all the time, and, although he really does take good care of his pet, we sometimes wonder if the poor thing wouldn't like to be free again—but "Tiny" isn't curious about that at all. Next year, in the Medical School, you want to keep your eye on this chap; he's about as mysterious as the guy with the smoked glasses.

name: Fortney, William Russell
motto: "Mr. Fortney, what do I mean when I say a man is six feet tall?"
description: MEET William Fortney, Dean of the Political Economy courses. "Bill" has about exhausted the list; he has tried them all, and where they have been found wanting has made the necessary addition. He has been hard at work for several months preparing a thesis on the often-asked question: "Mr. Fortney, what do I mean when I say a man is six feet tall?" "Bill" works on the theory that even if marks do vary inversely as the distance from the instructor, the safety secured by sitting outside the range of barrage fire is ample compensation. When questioned, this inhabitant of the wilds of Pennsylvania declared that the only thing that distinguished him from the Pennsylvania Dutch was the fact that the Dutch drank corn whiskey. General chorus, "Yeaaaa, Dutchman!" With those looks—see him in full dress, then, as the pilgrims to Mecca, destroy your sight; for you have seen the best in the country, and everything else will be in the nature of an anti-climax. "Bill" can't stop in the neighborhood of Charles and Twenty-third without blocking traffic with the crowd he attracts, so he takes a bodyguard—usually of one. We all like "Bill" for his wholesome smile and hearty laugh, and his really kind, if somewhat bantering, nature. Good luck, old boy!

name: Frank, Eli
description: Athlete (?), scholar and actor. The educated man, it is said, should know something about everything, and everything about one thing. Well, this about describes Eli; it is apparent that the one thing about which he has learned most is Dramatics. At last an actor! But let us recall this year's play, "If I Were King," and consider Eli's role. Ha, methinks there is something that we have not as yet learned about Eli, in view of the apparent ease with which he played the part of the inebriated scoundrel! Eli, where have you been keeping it? Have you been hiding behind the mask of Dr. Jekyll? Although he did not shine or cover himself with unusual glory on the cinder path, and though Phi Beta Kappa did not pledge him (another good man gone wrong), and he has not as yet received any alluring contracts from some of our leading theatrical producers, we do not wonder at his prominent position on the campus. We regard him as an all-round man, whose popularity is enhanced, and even dependent, upon his Frankness. Harvard will be as glad to see you next year, Eli, as we are sorry to part with you. You will be a credit to your profession, and, perhaps, the answer to some maiden's prayer.

name: Freeman, Aaron
description: Boys and girls, examine the exhibit closely, for here is Monsieur Aaron Freeman, Math "Shark" extraordinary, grandmaster of the Terpsichorean art, grammar and spelling bug, writer, and even poet—yea, the master mind of the E. E. Senior Class; the man with the smoked glasses, the boy wonder. Long before the comparatively slow-moving minds of his profs have finished explaining a deep point to some of his more dense fellow-students, Aaron is fairly writhing in his chair at the simplicity of it all, and howling for something hard to churn up in that aforementioned master mind of his. Aaron is far from being a "grind." He can be seen at every Hopkins athletic or social event, and is a staunch upholder of class spirit. It was through him and Collins that the Engineers' end of this HULLABALOO has been so well treated. His readiness to help others has gained him many friends, as has also his assortment of jokes. S'long, Kiddo! But, Aaron, please don't disprove any of these electrical theories, for we have had a hard time learning them, and it would give us heart failure to find them all wrong now.

name: Fulton, Richard Browne
motto: "All work and no play makes Jack a dull boy."
description: DICK has decided he will never be dull for the above reason, at least. Somehow or other, Dick has the knack of picking out that particular combination that radiates cheer in the dreariest kind of weather. It is a blessing that there was not a boy Pollyanna, or we would not be able to distinguish Dick from him. Yes, folks, Dickie is small but cute. Whenever you see a lighted cigarette, behind which shines a glowing countenance, with a hat for a background, you don't need to look for the rest (you couldn't find much more, anyway), because you already have all that you want of Dick Fulton. What are the secrets of Dick's happiness? First, work hard while you work, but don't give your pen a chance to run dry. Secondly, smoke plenty of cigarettes standing up against a radiator, and let the smoke fly out the top window. Third, keep your left hand in your pocket, except when driving what once was a Chevrolet. And last, but not least, see your best girl at least three times a week, and—oo-la-la. But Dick would be the same without all these recipes. He knows how to keep his worries to himself, and is just naturally one of the best-spirited chaps in our midst. By golly, it's hard to part with you, Dick, but they say all good things must end, so so-long.

name: Garvey, Joseph Michael
motto: "A thousand years less a day."
description: All kidding aside, however, Joe is a dandy little chap, and he sure is cute with the little mustache which he can raise in a day. In his "lower" classmen days Joe was known as a sure fire scrapper, and much remains of the pep which he instilled in his class during those trying times. But aside from being in for everything that would boost his class or University, he has always been such an excellent student that his classmates have frequently wondered how he obtained such good results with so little preparation. These attributes, together with his sociability and wit, have made him very popular, and we can do no more than offer him the toast which he himself has so often given to members of the fair sex: "A thousand years less a day."

name: Isadore Gerber
motto: "Way of the transgressor is hard."
description: Youth, beauty, charm—all are embodied within the remarkable personality of this promising Senior, destined, by virtue of his scientific bent, to become a medical researchist of note. With Gerber, it is but a matter of years; and after all, you know, the first 100 are always the hardest. Isadore has learned from bitter experience that the "Way of the transgressor is hard." Carelessness in Biology drawings spelled the ruination of his chances for honors, for Dr. C.'s official estimation of Gerber's work was noticeably inconsistent with the general grade of his marks. The sensitive youth could not understand this attitude on the part of the professor, and he could not to any greater degree restrain his self-assertiveness. Accordingly, he spoke, and hastily so, and hastily likewise did he emerge from the sacred precincts of the prof.'s office. Next to Biology, Chemistry, German and French, Gerber's main forte is Checkers. Regularly every lunch time at the Barn he can be found indulging in the manly sport. "Domestic" pool (i.e., he has his own table) receives the greater portion of his spare time at home. When the weather becomes too warm for such indoor sports, the Homewood tennis courts are graced by Isadore's presence. Such healthful pastimes constitute the secret of our "subject's" vigor.

name: Karl Gerber
motto: "I strove with none, for none was worth my strife; Nature I loved; and next to Nature, art. —W. S. Landor."
description: Karl is one of the most sincere and earnest students in our class. He aspires to become a chemist, but Fate has moulded him for a philosopher; for Karl dearly loves to philosophize, and if only one will lend a friendly ear, he will unburden himself with alacrity of his latest original interpretations of William James, Nietzsche or Bob Ingersoll. Karl holds the distinction of being the champion glass juggler of the lab., and rarely fails to catch the pyrex beakers on the first or second bounce. He has also developed a rare technique in pouring acid into water (especially recommended for those with extremely weak eyes). Karl is one of those chaps who must be known to be appreciated; and those of us who have been fortunate enough to "discover" him have learned some amazing things. A sincere, hard working, genial fellow, gifted with a keen sense of humor, he has been a distinct asset to the class, and we wish him the success he so richly deserves. Eighty-two

name: Haile, Leroy Yellott
motto: "Serrez La Main."
clubs: Assistant Instructor R. O. T. C., 3, 4
description: Roy is the grand old man of the C. E. class. Despite his advanced age he still skips about at a lively gait; upon being questioned he will lay this remarkable fact to his continuous course of exercise. However, the only exercise in which we have ever seen him engage was vigorously concentrated in the region of his wrist. L. Y. is a past grand master at this art, and is, with several others, responsible for the class motto: "Serrez La Main." The reason that Roy entered college so late in life is that he took a couple of years off in order to win the war. Like a good soldier, however, he is very modest about this achievement of his, and refuses to take all the credit. Roy does not intend to follow engineering, and as a result is not over-interested in the course. His chief hobby consists in daily counting the time remaining before that eventful night when he will be able to put forth his hand and receive the reward of his supposed labor, and for the first time look Prof. Pagon in the face without that hang-dog expression. Well, Roy, you have lots of company.

name: Robert Charles Hall
clubs: Corporal R. O. T. C., 2, and First Lieutenant, 3
description: This verse is more rhythmical than correct, for Bob really has a quite respectable soup-strainer. Indeed, it can be seen with the naked eye, and we have heard from the best authority that it tickles most adorably. Bob’s accomplishments, in addition to the noseguard, are so numerous that we cannot hope to do them justice in this limited space. A glance at the list above, however, will show that Bob has held almost every office from Captain of the Swimming Team to Class President. We must make some especial mention, however, of his financial ability. Bob has been treasurer of everything in the University with the exception of the Zionist Society and the Christian Service Club. We venture to say that more delinquent debtors have dodged Bob than a man with St. Vitus' dance could shake a matchstick at in ten years. We have a hunch that his engineering studies are a mere camouflage; his real ambition is to be a bank president. (Wall Street papers please copy.) If you don't owe money to any organization, Bob is the best of company and a darn good fellow. He is always in a good humor, has a good word for everyone, and is in for anything in the way of fun. In parting, Bob, we hope that some day we may be able to float a loan at your bank.

name: Hambleton, Richard Waldo
motto: "We'll tell the world he's got a good line. . . ."
clubs: Swimming Team, 1, 2, and H. A. A., 1, 2
description: A man of recognized talents and, we suspect, many hidden ones, is Dewey. We have been learning more about him every day for the past few years. We knew him for a lusty varlet when it came to heaving the weights for the Track Team, and for a merry knave, egad, with guitar or "uke" in hand. But the facility with which he selected the very softest job on the swimming team and then qualified for that job by casting records right and left, was an eye-opener. And his voice, that violet born to blush unseen, but for last fall's yell practice—that was another. Last, but not least, is the mysterious and dubious friendship which has recently sprung up between John and him. Yet on the whole, Dewey is a man of sense and discretion. He may go far; but we cannot see him doing so as a chemist. Our own idea of him in the future, though he does not welcome the suggestion, reveals him, richly colored with iodine, his noble girth enclosed in a natty grass skirt, his homely but honest countenance alight with the simple merriment of the primitive islander, twanging an "uke" in the snappiest native Hawaiian orchestra in the country.

name: Harden, Wilton Cope
motto: "For thy sake, tobacco, I would do anything but die." —Charles Lamb.
description: Hopkins, in the past, has laid claim to students from China, Japan, Russia, South Africa, and where not. But to this list must now be added Catonsville. The representative who places this city (?) in line with the above-mentioned foreign countries is Winnie. He came "clean" (Harry Jolson joke) from Catonsville to Hopkins in 1916. After a year of athletics he abandoned his college work to enlist in the U. S. Navy. When his term (in the Navy) expired in 1919, he re-entered Hopkins in the Engineering department. Winnie has been a familiar figure on the lacrosse field for the past two years. As a student of chemistry, he is without a peer, being a hard, consistent and conscientious worker. He may be located at the Chem. Lab. at any time between the hours of nine and five. Should the Anti-tobacco Bill pass Congress, we fear that science will lose a great man, for the glory of the combination of Harden and his pipe is strictly a 50-50 proposition. Therefore, say we, let's protect Science by voting down the Anti-tobacco proposal.

name: Stanley Edward Hartman
description: If one should delve into the Hopkins archives for the past four years, he would be astonished at the record of the above-pictured manly fellow, surnamed Stanley. This chap is without doubt one of the 57 wonders of the campus. His promptness and regularity of attendance at classes is one element that places him in a class all his own; his closely shaven face, red-tinted nose, and new modelled hat make him a fine specimen of culture. If a secret be in order, we might say that friend Eddie has the markings of a great football player, but because of his shoulder blade having been broken in practice, the millions that crowd Homewood each year have been robbed of even a glimpse of his brilliancy. Yet another secret; our Stanley might have been general in the R. O. T. C., had it not been for the fact that he disagreed with the Chief-of-Staff on various technical matters, and not wishing the Chief to resign, Eddie manfully stepped down and out. Edward has chosen the profession of law, and with his ability of formulating excuses on every possible occasion, without a hint of blushing, together with his pleasing personality, we are sure that success will crown all his efforts.

name: William Cullen Henry
clubs: 2nd Lieut. R. O. T. C., 3, and 1st Lieut., 3
Student Chapter A. S. M. E., 3, 4
description: In the fall of 1918 an unsophisticated country lad journeyed from the wilds of Kent Island to the great metropolis of Baltimore to partake of the fruits of learning. But, alas, he fell into the toils of that wicked organization, the S. A. T. C., and was unsophisticated no longer. Rumors of nightly expeditions to parts unknown, and of returns by the tunnel in the wee small hours, together with tales of his masterly cast of the bones (not as a minstrel), showed that he was fast absorbing the true college spirit. Even then, through the fall of the year his conversation was mainly of horses, plows, crops, etc.; but in the spring came a remarkable change. His trousers were always pressed, his hair slicked, and a brand-new mustache trimmed to perfection. The ladies, we noticed, were now given more than a passing remark. About this time "Hen" devoted himself seriously to the pastime of growing mustaches. In fact, he took such great delight in this that when he had one fully grown he would shave it off just to have the pleasure of growing a new one. In his studies he was inclined to take things easy until about two weeks before exams. Then he became the most worried fellow you ever saw, and would make all kinds of resolutions to study every night from then on, if only he should pass that set of exams. Aside from the charms of mustaches, "Hen" is well liked.

name: Len Henry
description: Journeyed from the wilds to partake of the fruits at a wicked organization. Rumors of nightly revelry in the wee small hours. As a minstrel, showed talent; then, through the fall crops, etc.; but in the end, always pressed, his hair. The ladies, we noticed.

name: Charles William Hess
motto: "Nobody loves a fat man."
description: Cupid, as he is known, because of his love-ly figure, is a living denial of the old adage: "Nobody loves a fat man." Every one likes "Cupe" because of his never-failing good nature. One of the mysteries of the Senior E. E. Class is how he gets all his assignments completed and in on time without ever seeming to do any work. He wanders in just before class time and fades away immediately after, seeming to float in and then fade away into space. "Cupe" is a great man with the ladies, and, consequently, scorned to take "Electricity and Magnetism" with the rest of the class, preferring to attend Dr. Bliss' class in Physics III, because that particular class was composed of three men and two co-eds. One of his peculiarities is the faculty of making many acquaintances, but very few close friends. However, those of us who are intimate with him can testify that to his real friends he is ever faithful and loyal to a degree that sometimes reacts to his disadvantage. He has not yet decided just what will occupy his time when he leaves college, although he does not expect to follow the engineering profession. We would not be surprised if he should drop into the medical profession, following in the footsteps of several of his family. If so, you can treat us, "Cupe."

name: Hildebrandt, John Lawrence
description: JOHN LAWRENCE has two choice possessions—a nickname and a mustache. "Hildy" is his nickname; that always sticks. The mustache comes and goes. The possession of this beautifier is not actuated by mere vanity, but, according to "Hildy" himself, by the usefulness to which it can be put. You see, when "Hildy" blows his cornet, the mustache acts as a bushing around the mouthpiece of his horn, thus preventing any undue air leakage. This bit of applied engineering shows that no mistake was made when "Hildy" chose engineering as a possible career. He himself claims, however, that his time spent at Hopkins will count for naught; that, as an engineer he will make a good house painter. But don't be misled by a man's opinion of himself. "Hildy" is already an authority on electric railway systems that operate on about 14 or 18 volts. In the laboratory he was very adept in making all connections and then sleeping through the entire test. When questioned by Dr. Lee, he never failed to give the proper answer, saying, in a monotone: "The third harmonic." Well, "Hildy," we've gibed you enough. Each of these remarks really represent one of your virtues. Your friendly spirit is something we hate to lose, and, whether you become a musician, motorcycle dealer, electrician, painter or banker (we'll see you often then!), your smiling, well-meant "hello" will remain with us always.

name: Bosley Hiss
motto: "I regard Mr. Hiss as the greatest living authority on 'Fish, and How They Sleep in Oyster Beds.'"
description: BOZ is essentially a man of culture, an idealist, a scholar and the possessor of an excellent sense of humor. He radiates complacency and good fellowship; it bubbles out of him in an effervescent stream like water from a volunteer fire company's hose. He likes to play bridge and talk about devilish things, but can't discuss Freud with a clear conscience. However, he knows all about literature, and can recite the works of Tennyson, Boccaccio, and William Jennings Bryan from start to preface. His recently acquired passion for Italian is a great mystery to us, but we have our own theories, which we shall not disclose. His lifelong friend, Dr. P. Greene, extols him as follows: "I regard Mr. Hiss as the greatest living authority on 'Fish, and How They Sleep in Oyster Beds.'" Boz made a very pleasant trip a few years ago over England, and thence through France and Belgium, until his money gave out. Then last summer he went up to New York and worked in a theater, taking the part of the hind legs of the beast in "Beauty and the Beast." These adventures mark him as both an explorer and an actor. He intends to waste his talents and go into business, where his ready wit should stand him in good stead.

name: Huey, Edward Greene
description: If anybody is needed to do some real work, and the job's too hard for the rest of us, we call on Ted, because we know that he is one who can be trusted to do well everything he undertakes. Some people are natural workers; he is not only one of these, but seems to have the power of accomplishing most, without any perceptible effort. Some day we're going to see him wearing a pair of boxing gloves, and carrying an armful of live eels at the same time—and nobody will be surprised. Like all other great men, Ted never makes much noise about things, but when he enters a room everybody knows he's there. We don't mean to say that he is averse to starting a riot by tapping his friends gently on the head with a chair; in fact, he breaks many pianos and such things when feeling in an especially playful mood, but he doesn't mean any harm by it. We regard Ted as one of the best in the class; one whose ability, foresight, energy, personality, and a score of other good qualities will help not only him but also those with whom he will be associated.

name: Eric Jacobsen
motto: Quietum animum mentemque benignam.
clubs: R. O. T. C., 1, 2, 3, 4, and Captain, 4
description: Imagine to yourself a golden-haired youth walking along with his hands in his pockets, and humming to himself about nothing in particular; just walking along and getting all the joy out of life that may be obtained by anybody with a clear conscience and a sunny disposition. Exercise your imagination a bit; let it dwell here and there on pleasing thoughts that arise, and you will gradually gain an idea of what Jake is like. He is our songbird, who lives for the pure joy of it all, and spreads his sunshine wherever he goes. What we meant to say was that Jake is the alligator's whiskers. He has formed the rare habit of thinking before he speaks, and it is very seldom that he fails to say the tactful thing. Nothing seems to bother him longer than a few minutes; he settles the question in his own mind, and forgets about it. He is not a reformer, but we take our hats off to him for his clean, straightforward manner and his ideals and conceptions of life. It is a wonderful thing to know someone who increases our faith in human nature, and we feel very grateful to him for all he has unwittingly done for us.

name: Christopher Johnston
description: Since he was old enough to have any ambition, Chris' one aim has been to become a doctor. He "cut up" so much that we fear he will be a surgeon, but at any rate, he takes great interest in biology and other kindred subjects. It is rumored that he tried a biological experiment upon his fraternity brothers one day, with the result that one perfectly good dinner was unappreciated by anyone save our hero himself. The Cotillion Club, the R. O. T. C. and the Swimming Team have occupied Chris' spare time at the University. He is always scheming some way to improve the Cotillions, and his efforts seem to have met with success this year. Chris is quite a ladies' man, and members of the fairer and weaker sex have been known to rave over his Apollo-like form when disguised in uniform or revealed in bathing suit. As a dancer he is the pride of the women and the envy of all, for few can equal him in grace or intricacy of maneuvers. His ability to work when it is time to work, and to relax at other times, coupled with a genuine interest and enthusiasm for his chosen profession is amply sufficient to give him a full measure of success. May good luck go with him!

name: Meyer Stanley Jolson
description: The mere mention of the name makes us think of the famous black-face comedian and singer, and investigations into his genealogical tree have finally convinced us that the two are definitely and closely related in blood. If ever the star of "Sinbad" could listen to the chantings of his cousin as he prayed (preyed) over the dead body of a feline, he would immediately exclaim: "Meyer, my boy, you are wasting your time in this foul smelling basement; come, come with me, and I will show you visions of boundless success on the stage." To finish this fantastic bit of imagined fact—Meyer quits the anatomy of the cat at the Epiglottis, bids farewell to his sorrow-stricken lab-mates, and taking the hand of his famous relative, departs from the sacred precincts of Dead Cat Alley. The animals, vegetables and fruits hurled at him at his first appearance cause him such a yearning for the quiet of Room 11 that he beats a hasty retreat, and returns to his old love. Another indoor sport with which Jolson has associated himself is the practice of teaching school. When the School Board required the services of a few Ichabod Cranes for substitute work Meyer exclaimed: "Five dollars a day is not such bad pay; and then, just to think, I get the Dean's excuse." Medicine or teaching; healing or corrupting—which?

name: Morris Keen
description: In truth, a Keen fellow. Our special limitations permit us to mention but a few of the ways in which our Flatbush friend has demonstrated to us the justification of his family name. First of all, a keen eye, as proved by the accuracy of his shots in disposing a la Jack Bentley of cats' livers and sheep's brains that have outlived their usefulness. Second, a "keen" dresser, as his charge account with Andy-Landy Co. indisputably shows (Morris' sport wardrobe is the best advertisement our progressive little shop ever displayed). Third, and that by which he is best known to us, a keen actor. Those who were fortunate enough to witness the splendid Dramatic Club production, "If I Were King," will, no doubt, refuse to believe us when we identify the above youth with decrepit old "Mother Villon" of the play. Yes, the two characters are one and the same, though just how far Keen had to alter his personality to adapt himself to the part we shall let those who know him determine. While not of the physical proportions of a Julian Eltinge, we feel that an "actress" endowed with such talent as he displayed ought to be capable of filling any role that might be assigned to him. And so we hope it shall be with him in his future professional life; fit and prepared to master any situation in which he may find himself.

name: Kent, Jesse Hall
motto: "You never can tell by the size of a frog, How far he is going to jump."
description: KID KENT, the boy from West Forest Park, is, indeed, petite, and we are inclined to believe he is a bandbox dandy. Especially would you think so had you gazed upon him the night of the Prom in his swallow-tail. Ha, boy! No corset ever fit the form more perfect. Can you imagine a fellow with an ambition? Well, he has one, and he says his being small does not make the world any smaller. It is his ambition to board a ship and sail the old world round. We don't know whether it's to see the ruins of Europe, the pigtail Chinese, or a girl in Honolulu, but the bet is safest on Honolulu. Hall is very bashful, but despite it all (or because of it?) he has made many friends. He'll lend you anything he has, whether it be a cigarette, Yukon Daintie, or his last shirt. If he hasn't got it, he'll buy it for you. Don't get the impression that Hall's friends are all confined to the strong sex. We know better. With his engaging personality and generosity, there is no telling to what heights this boy may climb, and our best wishes certainly go along with him.

name: Klotz, Benjamin Howard
description: Klotzie does not have to include in his several Art courses the art of conversing. A thesis on "How to Keep Your Partner Interested," by B. H. Klotz, or "My Formula for Social Success," would mean Easy Street to him for the rest of his life. Someone once bequeathed unto Klotz a horn, of the alto type, and immediately was he declared eligible for the Band; not that an alto horn is requisite to Emil's combination of instruments, but think how much Klotz was to enhance the appearance of the group by his commanding figure! A pianist (concerts held daily at noon in the Barn), a singer of note; what more, we ask, could a girl desire in a human man?

name: Andrew Herbert Knecht
clubs: Student Chapter A. S. C. E., 3, 4, and Vice-President, 4
description: ANDY is one of our big men—physically and mentally. Big enough to play tackle on our football team, strong enough to knock a baseball over the centerfield fence, and bright enough to win membership in Tau Beta Pi; such are the qualifications of the senior partner of the Andy-Landy Co. Andy began his career at Hopkins by playing centerfield on the Varsity team. His batting was a feature of that season, and has continued excellent throughout all four seasons. Last year found him at first base for a while; then the team became hard up for pitchers, and Andy was given a trial. Here he proved quite at home (he is half way home when he stretches), and rose to fame when, pitching his second collegiate game, he shut out the strong team of the U. S. Naval Academy by a 3-0 score. It has often been said that the combination of Andy's strength and Landy's skill could accomplish anything. Whether or not that be literally true, the fact remains that between the two of them they certainly did a wholesale business of the campus. Andy even found the selling game so enjoyable that he headed a season ticket campaign, which, by the way, was by far the most successful that Hopkins has ever attempted. As to his success in designing bridges, etc., we will not attempt to prophesy, but with his genial smile and strong personality he is certain to be successful in his life work.

name: Kuder, Bernard
motto:
"A youth there was of quiet ways,
A visage of the olden days."
description: As our next exhibit, we present to you a rather interesting specimen—one sometimes called cuter. Kuder is amongst us, but we scarcely know it. He talks on rare special occasions only, not even speaking when called upon to recite. His one peculiarity is that he always comes late to his first class, whether it be at 8:30 or 11:30. But Bernard has a great future—behind him. At the age of twelve, he constructed the original harp out of spools and picture-frame wire, and now his cherubic countenance has procured for him a place in the Mandolin Club. You can see him any Saturday night, full dress 'n everything, on his way to tantalize some unfortunate audience. As a hobby, Kuder has lately taken to cigarettes, and 'tis said he enjoyed both of them. Girls? Never again. Not since he overheard his best one telling her friend that "blushing Bernard is a bashful beau." However, a wagging tongue is an echo of an empty brain, so there's a world of hope for Kuder—may he always be cuter.

name: Langeluttig, Albert George
motto: "The kinks in my hair are nothing compared to the kinks in my brains"
clubs: Sgt., 2nd Lieut., 1st Lieut., 3; Capt., Lieut.-Col., General, 4
description: From infancy to premature maturity; from cap pistol to R. O. T. C. rifles; from a buck private in "Coxey's Army" to high potentate of the Ferocious Hopkins Devildogs, this lad's hair has eloquently earned him the name of Sunshine. Yea, verily, girls, this chap has "out-hennaed" henna. But, stand back and let not your hearts be so easily won, for in just the same manner that Syracuse and Penn have their unkissed heroes of the gridiron, Hopkins can boast of her unkissed Albert. This boy means business and hence has no time to flap with the flappers. (Poor Albert! Lucky flappers!) "But if you are looking for a long-time investment" — here it is. Between now and the time he develops his law practice, after exhausting the dusty volumes in a law school, any persevering girl can raise him to suit herself. But in order to save time we advise her to consult Dr. Mitchell regarding methods that have failed. However, be that as it may, for in much the same manner that some college wit has said: "The kinks in my hair are nothing compared to the kinks in my brains," we also say that Sunshine's tonsorial brilliancy is as night in comparison with his mental aptitude. The boy is clever, and we predict unqualified success for our glorious sunburst.

name: Henry James Lankford
description: Henry James hails from University of Richmond, from whence he came to join us in the fall of 1920. During his short stay with us, we have found one characteristic predominant; his alarming affinity for socials and any other chance of meeting with the fair sex. If any event is proposed to which ladies will be invited, or in which they participate, mark up H. James for at least two tickets. He practically refused to support this noble publication until he heard that Miss Betty was to be included. He is now a most ardent booster. When Henry first arrived at Hopkins his only desire was to study medicine; but when he had been here only a few weeks he discovered, as many others before him have done, that the pursuit of the knowledge requisite for the practice of medicine required time and study, and, of course, he was not built to meet these odious requirements. Henry then decided to study Pol. Econ., the course to which all finally come who, having a taste for all knowledge, have been unable to acquire real fervor for any particular subject. It is his desire to enter business with his brother in Norfolk, Va., after graduation, and, judging from the success of endeavors here, brother will have a nifty partner.

name: Lemmon, Norman Edward
description: At an early age he showed a decided tendency toward chemistry, in consequence, perhaps, of spending his childhood days in the fume-filled air of Wilmington. Here he entered high school, received 100 in chemistry, and was graduated. He then tried Delaware College, where he became President of the Chemistry Club. In 1920, realizing that there were still other worlds to conquer, he entered Hopkins as a Junior. Here he has faithfully served as the unofficial advisor to all Senior students in chemistry—even to the indefatigable Gerber. During his Junior year his chief amusement seems to have been teaching Hensel and Harden how to while away the hours of the E. E.
