Lab. period in hooking up motors and generators in many strange, yea, and diverse ways. To pierce beneath the skin, Lemmon's real ability is exemplified in his wonderful laboratory system—the despair of the profs. and the envy of his fellow-chemists.

name: Benjamin Szold Levin
description: We have with us today Benjamin Levin, the only true student that ever existed. Benjamin has extraordinary facility for picking out the driest and most difficult courses in the curriculum and, nevertheless, coming out on top. He just eats statistics, while Latin and Greek are his specialties. His favorite sport is chess, and he may frequently be seen atop the Barn, engaged in a thrilling fight to the finish. Although Benjamin is very fond of dead languages, we must not think that he is by any means a "dead one." On the contrary, he is an excellent entertainer; his greatest accomplishment is to arouse the sleeping sufferers in Pop Vincent's history class by working out chess problems or by writing love letters in Greek, while Pop discourses lengthily on the character of Pope Ambidextrus CXVI or the wives of Henry VIII. Benjamin is a great favorite with Bomb-us Mitchell, the famous labor leader, who immensely enjoys watching Benjie balance an open inkwell, a pencil box, and twenty-five books in one hand, and at the same time take notes with the other. Benjamin is likewise a famous athlete. He is the star of Dr. Abercrombie's Physical Torture Gang, and it is good for the soul to see him gracefully waving his arms on the plaza back of Gilman Hall, while the rest of the class looks on admiringly. Whatever Bennie selects for his life work we wish him a rousing success, and are sure he will ever be a credit to his Alma Mater.

name: Meyer, David Levin
description: Mike is a man who puts weight behind everything he says; in fact, he has accumulated over 200 pounds of avoirdupois. If might made right, Mike would get a grade of 10.5 in every subject. But somehow none of the profs seem to think that way. Mike also holds the undisputed record of being the only engineer on the campus who could not get into the power-house boiler through the manhole. If ever you see a crowd and hear one fellow arguing with about ten you can safely bet that Mike is in the center of the mess. Throughout his stay at Homewood he has been the target for quite a few jokes, and one has stayed with him till graduation. That was a mistake he made in the Mechanical Laboratory, when he put a carburetor on a Ford. The engine was started, and, behold, Mike had the thing feeding gas through the water pipe. He sent his idea to the maker of the worthy Lizzie, but it was too deep for Henry to see. Mike goes into everything with a rush. He plays baseball, basket-ball and takes a good deal of interest in outside athletics. We'll miss your robust figure, Mike, and with all thy faults, we love thy still.

name: Morton William Lieberman
description: LIEBE, as we are wont to call him, is a man of parts; that is, some parts of him are larger and stronger than other parts. It is supposed that he is heavier below decks than above, and from his seeming tenacity of adhesion to chairs and stools, the supposition bids fair to be implicitly believed. His main hobby is to see that our little Snookie approaches manhood in the proper way, under the most careful direction. Still, he also finds time to closely rival another member of our class in the line of wholesale purchases; the quality of some of his bargains is really remarkable for a man who apparently has neither business nor worries. In addition, we must admit that whenever there is class work to be done, Liebe is usually the man to do it on time. He seems destined to be an engineer, whether he so desires or not, for his family has saved him the trouble of deciding by agreeing long ago that it was best for him. We hope he will be as successful an engineer as he has been a student.

name: Milton M. Lilien
motto: "lets things come as they will come"
description: There is always to be found, among such a number of grinders, worriers, pessimists and trouble seekers, at least one who represents the brighter, rosier side of our life, and who "lets things come as they will come" and go as they will go. Mookie is our official gloom-disperser. When others are all "het up" over quizzes or exams, Mookie whistles away, hands in pockets, and tells all the Gloomy Guses to "go fall in the ash can" (pronounced ayish cayin). His whole philosophy is a challenge to the man who thinks that college is a place for long-faced and long-bearded individuals. To speak of Mookie's chief interests in life would entail a list of female names too long for our limited space; to enumerate his weaknesses would necessitate a repetition of the same list. Nor can we omit from the chronicles of the class the perfect record that he holds for attendance at the Century; if association with this place of amusement bears any relation to longevity, we can safely predict that Mook will be literally a true Century rounder. The little birds sat on the curb and chirped, For the world was bright and gay; And the Jersey cows on Thirty-third street Were glad that it was May. One Hundred and Seven

name: Frederick Wiessner Lipps
description: Cast your eye around the page and let it fall upon the beautiful face shining out above. We feel sure you will undergo an exceedingly pleasing sensation, which will be in no way diminished if you are so fortunate as to meet the original. In other words, Fred is a darn good egg, notwithstanding the fact that he hatches out a lot of trouble among the members of the contrary (and obstinate) sex. We are told he has them all in a card-index system, and makes dates by shuffling and cutting the cards. Sometimes he draws the Joker, seldom a spade. Fred doesn't talk very much except when he has something to say, and so he never has trouble in finding someone to listen to him. His chief interest has always been the University, although not necessarily the unimportant things, such as studies. Activities are his chief fields of endeavor, and he always lends a hand to everything that will further the interests of the college. He is to be found on the grounds at all odd hours, especially at night, but this is only because he takes night courses. Something tells us that our Fred is soon to be married, but we have our hopes that he won't narrow down the race to five or six for two years at least. It will take about that long for him to climb to the top of whatever ladder he may choose to scale.

name: Storrow, Gordon Lovell
motto: "But I would fain die a dry death." —Tempest.
description: Storrow has a personality that can accomplish much for him in succeeding years if he sees fit to turn it to his own advantage.  But throughout the time that we have known him we have never heard a chirp about his own troubles; he's always doing something for somebody else.  No matter how much or how little he may have, one-half belongs to you. We may conclude that Storrow will not be rich, but his open-hearted, generous disposition, and his unfailing sense of humor will bring him the priceless gift of a happy life.

name: MacDonald, Donald Hector
motto: "The bonniest lad that e'er I saw"
description: Mac is seriously suspected of being a bootlegger; if it is true, we have in him a mighty clever one at that. It does not take a keen observer long to notice that Donald showered considerable attention upon the fair attendant of the chemical stock room (how about those Nigger Baby candies?), and associating this with the fact that a vast supply of alcohol is kept under her watchful eye, we have at least a clue that unquestionably would make things look black for Mac in any court room. Our own theory is that he personally is as temperate as Father Bryan, but that imbibers of hootch, taking advantage of his pull at the stock room, have made Mac tempting offers. And so, if Mac really is a bootlegger, those are the facts. If he is not, no harm done, n'est-ce pas, Mac? We had to work on the hypothesis, "the Scotchman loves his whiskey," and, therefore, if we have been disillusioned we can only conclude that Mac is not loyal to his ancestral heritage, though we know he will be to the medical profession, in which we look for a rapid rise into prominence.

name: Herbert John Mahle
description: Here we have one of the bright fellows of the E. E. Seniors; they're as scarce as hen's teeth. Drop in some day and you are likely to hear: "Herb, how do you do this?" from all four corners of the room. It is indeed fortunate for some of us that Herb is so generous and good-natured. His sweet temper must have been absorbed by the other members of his laboratory group, for they have never been known to argue. We never could understand why Herb, although apparently bashful in the presence of ladies, makes such a hit with them. Is it his ability to keep one eye shut? Ah, we have it—this blond Adonis from Howard Park can put to shame the very founder of the Terpsichorean art. Seldom does his love song burst forth, but when it do, Oh, how it do! Herb is also very touchy, and thereby hangs a tale. We all say that Herb is a man of means, because he owns a car—beg pardon—we mean a galloping showcase. Folks, it's a regular hacking car, only the hacking is more prominent than the cash. Herb, with your precision, brains and generosity, you're going to buy the U. S. some day, so why worry? We'll all be in the poorhouse before you fail of success.

name: Charles Clagett Marbury
description: Charlie is a more or less serious chap, who has a rather definite idea of what he wants to do about most things, and who carries out his plans, no matter what happens. He has his work figured out so well that he knows just exactly how many nights a week he can play bridge without hurting his average. Most of us think we can do this fairly well ourselves, but we're raw recruits when compared with Charlie. There is something about him that attracts the ladies; he spent some time in France helping General Pershing, and they do say those French girls—. Furthermore, we all like him; we enjoy his company and genial spirit, and we certainly are appreciative of his jokes. With that preciseness which is so characteristic of him, Charlie lights a wheezing pipeful of his Sulphur and Linen mixture, shuts his eyes a few minutes, and then proceeds with a new line of jokes each time. We can truthfully say that we are glad to have had Charlie as one of our number, and hope that everybody else will find him as good a companion as have we during our all too brief period of companionship.

name: Markell, John Usher
description: EVERY TIME John thinks of a bed he begins to feel sleepy, and every time he feels sleepy he thinks of a bed. He used to hang an alarm clock over his head, but soon fooled himself by forming the habit of turning off the alarm in his sleep, when it rang in the morning. It is a different story when there is a game of football. If there is a hole in the line an inch wide, John goes through it; if the line is solid, John uses the simple method of pushing it back out of his way, and forgetting all about it. Another peculiarity is that he hits harder as the game progresses. In addition to his athletic prowess, we have found him to be a wonderfully likeable chap and a true friend, with all the qualities of a gentleman. He will probably retire to the wilds near Frederick and raise apples, although he doesn't say whether they will be for eating or—well, you know. Get up, get up, you sleepy head! You are a dreadful sinner. We need those sheets for tablecloths, For now 'tis time for dinner. One Hundred and Thirteen JHU

name: McComas, James Anderson
motto:
"Duck, mother, here comes the gas man."
—Olde Ballade.
description: To the casual observer, Mac would appear to be a quiet, home-loving boy. But appearances are often deceitful (Shakespeare), and you can't tell a book by its cover (Geo. M. Cohan). So it is with Mac. One night he was discovered at Mount Holly, where he was surely shaking a fragrant sock with all the air of an authority on the art of Old Saint Vitus. However, this is not his only accomplishment by any means. As an analytical chemist, Mac has made quite a hit with one of Prof. Gooch's disciples, and as a B. T. U. chaser in his work for the above advertised company we feel that he has a brilliant future ahead of him. At least, he will help to bring brightness into many a home.

name: Thomas King McCubbin
motto: "Old King Cole was a merry old soul."
description: WHO says that there is nothing in a name? King McCubbin could give His Highness of the nursery rhyme three laughs handicap and still win in a walk. His optimism and cheerfulness are dimmed but twice a year—just before examinations. During the week or two before these modern instruments of destruction fasten their grip upon the body of John S. Hopkins, Mac is a picture of despair. A casual listener would suppose that Mac never had passed an exam, never expected to, and in the case of the ones in question had not the slightest hope in the world. He mourns so convincingly that we believe him, and when the marks are posted we look first to learn the worst about ourselves, and then to see how many conditions Mac received. Then comes the surprise. Fully expecting to have to console Mac, we find that he has gone through in safety, and is in a position to console us. How come, Mac? At any other time of the year, however, King is so full of optimism and good humor that he is quite cherubic. His wit is unfailing and keen, and his play on words a joy to the student of A. C. Theory. Last, but not least, Mac has a girl. Note that this symbol of tenderness is used not in the plural form, as would be necessary for most of us, but with an emphatic singular. We don't know what she looks like, but from Mac's description she must be a wonder; so here's to the King and Queen, and may their Highnesses reign forever. One Hundred and Fifteen

name: Milligan, Robert Fisher
motto: "good luck."
clubs: Treasurer Student Chapter A. S. M. E., 4
description: Here we have one of the hardest working men in the M. E. class, and, from his picture, one of the handsomest. Now, we're not insinuating that Millie is not a fine-looking chap, with his blond hair and blue eyes (for he's all that and then some), but it took several sittings and a couple of hours of persuasion on the part of the photographer to get the above result. In his three years here at Hopkins, Bob has made much school history. In his Freshman year he was one of those who made the Sophomores shudder. We don't know yet whether that look that Millie carried on his face (it was half of suffering and half of ferocity) caused the Sophs to spare him because they feared or actually pitied him. At any rate, he surely did take it out on the next year's Freshmen. The look was all ferocity then. Bob has a varied athletic past. First, he played quarterback on Kistler's Physical Wrecks. This experience helped him greatly in holding the same position on the M. E.'s successful (?) football team. Millie is also a member of the leather-lunged track artists, and has pressed much of Homewood soil in his cross-country jaunts around the campus. (Yours for a "trackless" campus.) Then, as for indoor sports, the machine used by the Seniors to run the Jazz Boiler Test belonged to Bob, who originated the idea. Well, Bob, you'll probably get along as well on the outside as you did in here, so "good luck."

name: Mullikin, James Clayland, Jr.
description: In Mr. Mullikin we have an anomaly, a short-haired litterateur, one of those endemically Menckeneze persons of marked atavistic tendencies. While his despicable brethren are excogitating, perspiring (sweating is too vulgar), ruminating (literal) on the variations of plants and animals under domestication, and the single tax and the unearned increment, this esoteric gentleman spends his time browsing in Cytherean pastures and perusing the sentient, mauve-colored works of Dreiser and Hergesheimer. To him all litterateurs are as one. He knows as much about Flaubert, Mallarmé, and Arthur Rimbaud as the average college professor (a very low genus) knows about Pater, George Eliot, and Robert Browning. Goethe, Schiller, Hegel, Ariosto, and Schopenhauer are mere bagatelles to him. Dr. Mullikin hails from the Eastern Shore, "The Country God Forgot." Immediately, the "American" secured his valuable services and efforts. Today, Mr. Mullikin is the foremost reporter in his microcosm. He is, I think, ingenuous, if not innocent. Taken all in all, he is a good son, a good husband, and a good father. Mullikin intends to go in for "belles lettres," and we all know that some years hence he will be castigating the hoi polloi and whatever is left of the bourgeoisie with his monoacidicadisterric pen, and all we can humbly hope is that none of his verbal accolades (ironical) fall upon our pure and nacreous (Amy, please notice) brows.

name: Muth, Robert Clementian
motto: Cherchez la femme.—Fr. Proverb.
description: This man, called R. C. by his more intimate friends, is as well known as the guardian angel (M. E. joke, not the Ed.'s poor spelling), who keeps watch over the scholastic honors of the M. E. Seniors. Throughout long years of trial and privations, R. C. has kept them to the straight and narrow path, away from the deadly legions of the Blacklist. We dare add that only the finest reasoning and great patience of a fiery genius could do such a thing. Of late, however, we have noticed a drop in Muth's scholastic standing, and upon rigid investigation have found that he is now a devotee of the little god Terpsichore, before whom he nightly offers his sticks of joss, to the tune of weirdly clashing cymbals, and bells sweetly reminiscent of bovine lowing. R. C. has always kept his private life closed to the world, but, judging from his extremely abstracted air, unusually neat, not to say nifty, appearance, and early departure after classes, we are led to believe that there is a "woman in the case." Of course, we have no definite grounds for the charge, but—, well, figure it out for yourself. Why should a quiet engineering student, a Tau Beta Pi, suddenly be possessed of a desire to trip the light fantastic?

name: Morris Myers
motto: "The memory of the righteous is a blessing."
description: They all flop sooner or later! Before many moons have passed, we predict, this shy youth, who once averred that his pale lips were untouched by female osculating organs, will have succumbed to the charms of some bewitching little maid. A moonlight night, a painful silence interrupted by emotional sighing, a murmur, and then—. May Heaven forgive us for this wicked thought. Yes, 'twill all be over, and our present head of the unkissed fraternity will have sold his vicinity for a "miss" of pottage. Morris, despite his desire for work, has tolerated throughout the past year the corrupting discussions of the Disciples of Oscar, who met regularly in Room 11 every Monday and Tuesday afternoon, from 1 to 4 (Myers, 1 to 5); and if this modest son of Virginia misses his Phi Beta Kappa key we shall attribute it only to the distraction he suffered from pondering over the ideals of the above-mentioned famous character. Myers' career at college constitutes a splendid example of what may be the reward of a man who allows the girls to live without offering them the least molestation. Much the richer in knowledge than the rest of us; by far the more opulent in marks; happier, no doubt, by reason of his immunity to the "Lieberregenden Bacterien," does Morris emerge for the last time from the tunnel of Gilman Hall.

name: Naviasky, Joseph Abraham
motto:
"The muffled drum's sad roll has beat
The soldier's last tattoo."
—Theodore O'Hara.
description: He deserves special mention for his work as chairman of the Junior Banquet Committee, and as official custodian of the funds of the Senior Class. In this latter capacity, Navvy has proved to be all that an ideal treasurer should be. Time-consuming as these official duties must be, they do not prevent him from carrying out an excellent series of Goochsonian experiments. His future work will probably be in the field of chemistry, and we feel confident that his talents will soon be recognized in a very satisfactory and remunerative way. Editorial Note: It has been a subject of debate whether Navvy will become a Chemical Engineer or a "Breakman." This discussion has arisen from his persistent attempts to remove all glassware from the stockroom shelves.

name: Edward Choate O'Dell
description: Snookums is the dark horse of the C. E. Department. Although an angel in appearance and actions between 8:30 A. M. and 4:30 P. M., during five days of the week, we have heard that during his leisure hours he is anything but a saint. In fact, it is reported that he is one of the Board of Governors of the F—lly Theatre; his special duty is that of chorus inspector and manager. Another suspicious circumstance is that he plays the organ in church on Sunday morning. After thoroughly analyzing these facts, reports and rumors, we reach the surprising conclusion that it is beyond the power of human understanding for anyone to judge correctly his true character. Fair, of medium stature, pleasant to look upon, neatly dressed, Snookums at all times suggests innocence personified. But you never can tell. Indeed, his classmates have found him an eminently learned authority on the writings of Boccaccio, and occasionally he has even been known to recite some of the gems of this author. Altogether he is a very likeable chap; is easily kidded—which, needless to say, has furnished much amusement for the other C. E.'s—though he takes this kidding in good part, and has returned joke for joke. He has held his own with ease; may he be equally successful "outside."

name: O'Keefe, John
motto: "Philologists, who chase a panting syllable through time and space, start it at home, and hunt it in the dark, to Gaul, to Greece, and into Noah's Ark." —Cowper.
description: He is an excellent linguist, and we trust that his dawning financial career will be as successful as his stay at J. H. U.

name: Owings, Robert Edward
description: BOBBY needs but one office to insure his absolute success in baseball—President of the Committee on Rules. If he could just reduce the number of innings in a regulation game from nine to six, or even seven, the boy would be right there. As it is, there are few better half-game pitchers. The fault is not lack of nerve, but just a mistake of a usually benevolent nature. We have always had a sneaking suspicion that if someone would knock off Bobby’s hat and muss up that scrupulously neat mop of hair, the former Friends School star would become so hot that he would pitch both games of a double-header. Want to try it, Armie? We also find him captain of the only undefeated basketball team in the history of Hopkins athletics. The minor detail that no games were played does not detract from this record one bit. To do him justice, though, Bobby has showed himself to be a versatile and capable athlete. He is the only three-letter man in the University at the present time. He made his letter in football the first year, and while still a Freshman produced the goods in basketball and baseball, to which activities he has since devoted most of his time. Bobby is “quiet and unassuming” to an extent that disguises his real worth; but we who know him well proclaim him a good sport and a capable man.

name: Parsons, Paul S.
description: Now it came to pass that a number of Sophomores did betake themselves one evening to celebrate the Feast of the Passage of Examinations, whereat there was much joy among the youths and maidens, and they did feast and pet and shake the wicked shimmy. But there was among them one who arose and issued forth a proclamation, saying: "Know ye, that I am of the tribe of Freshmen, and I care not a damn for whoso calleth himself a Sophomore." Whereupon the Sophomores were filled with anger, and sware among themselves, yea, even among themselves sware they to administer punishment to the offender. At the eleventh hour did they carry him up away hence with them into the land called Homewood, and did suffer that he be baptized thrice in the Lake of Jeremiah, of the tribe of Smallwood. "Pash" claims that the moon never sets on any of his female admirers. Girls, girls, everywhere, from Florida to Maine, from Italy to the bonnie Highlands, and thence to Hagerstown. Statistics show that if they could all be brought together in a room of sufficient size, the World War would dwindle into insignificance. Paul also plays lacrosse and has made his mark on several opposing players, who have cause to remember his impulsive stick work and generous love taps. Once in a while he studies, and is gifted with an uncanny power of absorbing a lot in a very short space of time. (Read that sentence again.) Anyhow, he's a nice boy, and we like him.

name: Platt, Edgar Gillmore
description: Ed might well be called a college tramp. Before entering our own far-famed emporium of knowledge, he graced, for a period of two years, the hallowed halls of the University of Pennsylvania. From thence, harkening to the importunate call of Minerva, he hied forth for a while to Carnegie Tech. Then, finally, having arrived at years of discretion, he entered the Class of 1922. A man of parts, forsooth! In spite of his late start, Ed became quickly acclimated. He learned to nod his entire approbation upon being solemnly assured for the third consecutive time that "Business men are hopelessly unacquainted with economic theory." Then he learned to register incipient delight at the inevitable "And then I said to Elihu—." But if the profs ever try to call his bluff—presto—they find there is none. Ed is a serious, capable student, who, believing that you get out of a course only what you put into it, has determined to put enough into all his studies to make them really worth while. All of which augurs well for his success in a smug, material world. At all events, the best of luck to a quiet, good fellow—a gentleman and a scholar, truly.

name: James Francis Porter
high school: Allegany County High School, Cumberland, Md.
description: Well, folks, this is Francis. No, he's not the "Francis that dances with me, hully gee"; on the contrary, he is far from being a cake-eater. He has even confided that he'll never get married, but, of course, we must take that with a grain of salt. You see, whenever Porter and Hildebrandt go to an affair together, each escorts the other's lady friend home. Co-operation is the watchword of these army officers. This tall friend of ours is one of the most conscientious workers in the class. His work was always done weeks ahead of time, and still he was as busy as a bee. His co-workers always had to slow him up, for if you ever let him get so far ahead of you that he got the notion he was being overworked, then—good night! You thought a blizzard had struck you, and, the first thing you knew, you were working without Porter. Then, shortly, you would go back to him rather timidly and ask for his advice. You couldn't possibly get along without him. One of Grandpap Porter's favorite hobbies was to tell hayseed witticisms, and the other kind, i. e., a la "Black and Blue Jay" (we'd better get a good writeup from that magazine now). His yarns on railroad riding in Western Maryland afforded us many an interesting hour. Porter pulled us all out of many a hole, for who else would dare call time on the Dean? With your diligence and nerve, Frank, old boy, we sincerely believe that you're going to deliver the goods.

name: Price, William Albert Jr.
motto: "I am two per cent genius; I am ninety-eight per cent perspiration." —Edison.
description: Noise might frighten a burglar; noise might cause the separation of friends; noise might make a class banquet; but noise never made a man. The consequence is that this man prefers to think much and talk little. If silence will make a man, surely we have in this fellow the making of a great one. Always silent, but always working and planning his future life's work. This young man is one of those individuals whose great inner motives—which are the perpetrating forces of human hope and endeavor—have not yet manifested themselves. Modesty is a virtue, unless it keeps us from enjoying the best fellowship, such as is found in the association with true friends. Since we do not know the tendency, or motive, which so habitually causes this lad to be the silent but frequent occupant of the northwest corner of the library, let us hesitate to call him modest. Despite the fact that he is a hard worker, there still lies beneath his peaceful exterior a warm heart for every fellow at Hopkins. His fondness for good music, and his temperate spirit have combined to make him a friend to all, with malice toward none. We wish him much success and happiness in his work at Princeton Theological Seminary in preparation for a benevolent ministry.

name: Putzel, Edward Lewis
motto: "Up! up! my friend, and quit your books; Or surely you'll grow double; Up! up! my friend, and clear your looks; Why all this toil and trouble? —Wordsworth."
description: Were the ancient Egyptians to write the name of my subject it would possibly take the form of a desk in the main library of Gilman Hall, with a student, not very tall, pouring over a book till 5 P. M. any day. A necessary feature of our pictogram is a tan brief case bulging with books. Why the young gentleman carries his entire library about with him is difficult to imagine; since, however, the species under consideration is rare, i. e., never gets less than 9.5, we of the under classes cannot but envy the efficaciousness of this procedure. Putzel would be a speaker and a politician. In the former line he has persevered and at last attained some eminence; but in the latter line of endeavor our hero has fallen by the roadside, in spite of the splendid training he is receiving in the Dean's classes (sarcasm). We must give credit where credit is due. Putzel has succeeded in obtaining good marks and showing his pre-eminence not in "snap" courses, but in subjects difficult and little compatible with his natural trend of mind.

name: William Alexander Randall
clubs: President J. H. U. Student Chapter A. S. C. E.
Sergeant in Battery E, M. F. A.
description: It is still in doubt as to whether or not the Book of the Fates declared that twenty-three years ago the world would be enriched by a great military genius. The fact remains, however, that at that time William Alexander Randall first made himself heard upon the earth, and it is said that the Kaiser immediately made preparation to conquer the world before Bill's genius finally matured. From Major of his prep school battalion to Sergeant in Battery E, M. F. A., was the first step, and in this capacity he served during the World War, seeing active service and fighting in the battle of Vimy Ridge. At the close of hostilities the lure of higher education again asserted itself, and the Sergeant turned to Civil Engineering. It is true that as yet his name has not become known in engineering circles, but it is reported that a large number of contracting concerns have already put in bids for the erection of a trans-Atlantic bridge, with a two-thousand-mile span—the result of the latest idea of this promising young engineer. But Bill is rather indifferent about these, and, in fact, all other University activities, with the exception of the Cane Club. In this he puts forth his best endeavors, and with his excellent appearance and personality bids fair to spread the fame of his organization to the furthermost points of Gouch-er. One Hundred and Twenty-nine

name: Herbert Eilert Reifschneider
description: The first six months of each college year are, to Reify, as unnecessary as the buckles on a pair of galoshes, for it is not till about April 1 that anything of interest is opened up to the ambitious undergraduates (sez Reif). Football, swimming, and basketball are but preliminary to the advent of the baseball season, and it is this time of the year that brings joy to the heart of our old standby, Herb. The secret of his success on the Hopkins diamond may be traced to the coincidence that both he and Coach Armstrong are possessed of the same front name, thereby giving him a drag not enjoyed by other members of the team. In anticipation of the ball season, Reify suffers a long spell of spring fever, extending throughout the winter; and it was only after much moral "suasion" on the part of Rush that he gathered enough pep to attend the Senior Prom. Once there—ask her, she knows. With the baseball season under way, however, Herb shows 'em all how to scoop up the grass cutters and "click the old onion," as he puts it. Some day, in the near future, "Babe" Ruth, in search of material for the New York "Yanks," will hear of this Towson (or Timonium) star, and, smash! will go Reify's medical ambitions before the dazzling offer. Don't let them kid you, old boy!

name: Rones, Benjamin
description: It is, in truth, a pity that space will not permit life-size photographs of the Sheiks that adorn the pages of this HULLABALOO, for, in this instance, in particular, would we be accorded a treat as rare indeed as the phenomenon of a 10 in Physics. Though we have never seen our caveman in battle array, we know from one little incident on the campus (P. S.—Jake Miller) that he is the real Jack Dempsey of the class. Though, physically speaking, a powerful man, "Smiling Ben" is as tender hearted as a lamb, and that fact satisfactorily explains why he never made his mark on the college gridiron. The weakness of his opponent would, at all times, move him to pity, and he found himself utterly helpless against the dictates of his big heart. Rones has decided to specialize in diseases of the female heart—first, heart-breaking, and then, by lengthy treatment, heart-mending; a malpractice, to be sure, but in the eyes of the caveman an honorable and highly profitable one. Some day, we predict, Sargent, the famous artist, will be requested to add a fifth figure to his celebrated canvas, "The Four Doctors" (now exhibited in Gilman Hall Library). This fifth figure, ranking along with Osler, Welch, Kelly and Halstead, will be none other than "Big Ben," disguised under a still longer hirsute growth, looking down with classic gaze upon the awe-stricken, reverent "Frosh" of future generations. One Hundred and Thirty-one

name: Roschen, Ernest C. Henry
motto: "An honest soul i' faith, sir, by my troth he is, As ever broke bread. —Much Ado About Nothing."
description: ERNEST is known to his intimates as "Hunk." Whether his nickname is derived from some phrase like "a hunk of flesh," or from "hunkie-dorie," we have been unable to ascertain. Coming from either it would be entirely appropriate, for this Baron of Reisterstown is inclined to be roly-poly, and is certainly a jolly fellow. He claims a distaste for the weaker sex, but Dame Rumor hath it that a certain fair maid from Reisterstown plays an important part in "Hunk's" activities. We would almost say "another man gone wrong," but, of course, we don't know the girl, "and that," as "Hunk," we feel sure, would say to himself, "makes all the difference in the world." But strange as it may seem, Ernest is a good scholar, and has a high average in his lessons; strange, we say, for it has often been observed, yea, even by the poets, that love and lessons go but sadly as partners in college. Ernest Carl Henry, though a quiet person, is well liked by those who have had the chance to come in contact with him, and we wish him every success in his graduate work next year in Geology.

name: Max Harry Rosenfeld
description: It really "Max" no difference in what manner we here perform the sacred duty of our office, for the editor and his official staff of razzers are in for a suit of libel for whatever they attach to this page of the HULLABALOO. Our subject is, to all intents and purposes, a lawyer in the making, and we venture to predict that his very first legal experience will consist of defending himself in the case: Rosenfeld vs. 1922 HULLABALOO. If we, with our limited means of oral attack, are to be opposed by the fire of this practiced speaker, then we look pessimistically at the outcome. Was not Max a participant in all contests of oratory held during his residence at the University, failing to win each time only because there were others entered in the competition? What, for example, could Attorney Rosenfeld enumerate as causes for his instituting legal proceedings against us? The mentioning of a girl named—— ; an intimation that we suspect him of having once allowed his upper lip to remain unshaved in an effort to emulate a mustached member of the staff; reference to the fact that he suddenly, for some inexplicable reason, substituted Law for Medicine as his chosen calling. From the above the reader can readily form his own opinion as to the propriety or impropriety of writing as we do.

name: Rosenfeld, Solomon Burnett
motto: "A different girl a day keeps trouble away!"
description: Yea, green sweater,—ha, ha, ha! Ye gods, Rosie, what made you pick out that ungodly color! Well, anyway, we don't guess we can blame you for wanting to show your nationality. S'all right, kid, no harm meant. About three years ago the addressee, who signs himself Solomon Burnett Rosenfeld, set out to conquer the distant realm of Homewood. Instead of conquering, Rosie succumbed to the wiles of the Philistines, and degenerated into a normal, mild, Hopkins Freshman. When it comes to the female sex, Rosie is at his best. If Rosie believes that a rolling stone gathers no moss (girl), present indications are that he will become a venerable old bachelor. Rosie has yet to be seen with the same girl twice, and the funny thing about it is that he claims he likes 'em all. The only material result of his experiences is the compilation of a directory containing the names and addresses of practically every girl over 13 years of age, in Baltimore and vicinity. Should you require information about any girl, just get Rosie to look her up in his little red notebook. Women are really a side issue with Rosie. Give him a gas engine with plenty of grease, and he'll think he is in Heaven. But even though Rosie is a regular auto mechanic, he is a most admirable chap—always smiling, never worrying.

name: Brafman Rosenstock, Benjamin
motto: "One of the first principles of decorative art is that in all manufactures, ornament must hold a place subordinate to that of utility." -- George C. Mason.
description: Ben is a quite serious chap, a hard worker, but no grind. He is one of the elect who, you know, has just got to make good. Why he insists on putting so much of his time to studying the why's and wherefore's of Art and Archaeology we can't possibly imagine, for we know Rosie expects to run his father's big canning factory up in Frederick. However, if Ben's researches will give us more artistic tin cans, we are satisfied. L'Art super alles. Rosie is also quite a geologist on his own account, and spends his summers mapping out the streets of Frederick. This summer he expects to travel to Alaska and plot out a few suburbs for the overcrowded cities. We wish you luck, Ben, and expect to find your name one day among the most illustrious of our alumnae.

name: Davidge, Harrison Rowland
motto: "you can't keep a good man down."
description: DAVY is an all-around man, a lion among women (when he gets started), a seal in the swimming pool, and one of the best students in the Engineering School. It is a rare combination much to be admired, though seldom found, and all the more desirable when we say that Davidge hasn't any signs of a swell head. Just before exams his spirits are always depressed, but, after all is over, we find him still in the University, and nowhere near the Blacklist. And in the spring when he steps out with a cane—oh, boy—keep an eye on your girl, for his line is deadly, and his appearance much in his favor. As an athlete, Davy has done much for his Alma Mater. Six years ago he began to break breast stroke records in the B. A. C. tank, and after winning this event some time later, while in the A. E. F., he returned to Hopkins to become captain and later manager of the Black and Blue mermen. Not satisfied with this record, he put in a year on the football squad, and two years on the tennis team. He also acted as a Senior Delegate to the Student Council, and has held numerous minor offices. Keep an eye on him, fellows, for "you can't keep a good man down."

name: Rudel, Charles Joseph
description: CHARLIE is one of those boys who usually comes to an 8:30 class late. This lateness is very regular in that it occurs the same mornings in every week, but he has a very good reason: some of us wish we had such a reason. Charlie's reason resides at——. Charlie's greatest hobby is worrying. We have never known him to take a quiz that he did not worry as to its outcome, and when the results are posted, he cannot understand how his mark came to be so high. This boy is also some little arguer, and many is the time he has been threatened with assault and battery by fellow-occupants of the E. E. Seniors' room for carrying on these arguments in tones above a whisper; yea, very much above a whisper. Then, when you're going home, Charlie will yell: "Wait a minute!" Thomas and Weikel, the E. E. fresh-air fiends, are Charlie's deadly enemies. No sooner does one raise a window than he lowers, and he always blames it on Burnham! Yes, Charlie, if you stay home enough to get some sleep, you will be President of the Western Electric Company. They need a man who can worry conscientiously. One Hundred and Thirty-seven

name: Rush, Richard Irwin
description: We think that Rush has, perhaps, made a mistake in his profession. If ever he decided to go out for baseball, he would certainly make his mark as a receiver, a position in which he has had much experience. He has been on the receiving end of the ticket line at Homewood Field for several years, during which time he has not made an error; he held everything that came his way. When not in his luxurious quarters at the Field, Rich spends most of his time examining the innermost secrets of Karcharias, at which he is quite a shark. He also ably assists Dr. Gardner in reducing the feline population of Baltimore and the vicinity. Whether the gaseous protest to which these latter animals ultimately resort has led him, disappointed in the limited English vocabulary, to supplement it with a choice assortment of foreign expletives, we do not know. This much, however, is certain: Rich is on speaking terms with French, and being a generous fellow, he does not intend to keep this knowledge to himself, so next year will find him teaching. Speaking of generosity, Rich has a big heart, and wouldn't hesitate to share his choicest possessions with his friends.

name: Schneider, David
motto: "May all your pain be sham pain, and all your champagne real."
description: How pleasant is Saturday night when you've tried all week to be good! That is the song sung by Dave Schneider, whose cherubic features and jolly countenance are herewith reproduced. (No, girls, he has had his phone taken out.) All week David stays at home, not studying, however, as he gives his folks to believe, but, in reality, figuring and planning how to spend the following Saturday night, and how to improve on the one passed. The pursuit of such tactics is a likeable, easy-going chap, possessing that quality for which great nations have warred and bled—democracy. David is, to be sure, democratic of spirit. Why, he would bum a cigarette from a Freshman just as soon as from a "grad." No airs about that young man! As a result of such tactics, cigarette boxes vanish from view when this astute Senior approaches, and more than once has poor democratic Dave been forced to buy his own fags. Sometimes Dave assumes a most serious mien, and we are wont to believe that he at one time was the victim of a disappointment in love, and that any memory of his former bliss causes a shadow to flit across his cheery physiog. But don't worry, old man, "May all your pain be sham pain, and all your champagne real."

name: David Edward Schuchts
motto:
"Pains of love be sweeter far
Than all the other pleasures are."
description: Ladies and gentlemen (particularly the ladies), gaze ye upon David Schuchts, the personification of beauty. Them eyes, those nose, and that lips—how we ground-grovellers do envy them! Coming back to earth, we find Dave a most versatile chap. He is the only engineering student in our midst who fully appreciates lectures foreign to his course. He delights in listening to a lecture on deadology, and rides the rest of his classmates for being so narrow-minded. On the other hand, his proclivities for the absorption of electro-magnetic and transient waves astound the Dean himself. One thing about Dave—you can never scare him. That "laissez-faire" attitude of his just has no equal. He believes the whole world as honest as himself, for he'll go home leaving his desk unlocked and everything piled up on top. Dave is always ready for a smoke, a trip to the Barn, or a show downtown. Truly, he is a man of leisure, and has never been known to worry. How he has time for all this and can still finish his work is beyond us, but he nevertheless manages to do it. Despite Dave's versatility, he really likes engineering and even aspires to head the A. T. & T. Co. some day. You'll do it, Dave, provided you retain that attractive personality of yours.

name: Schulze, Ivan Leonard
description: In spite of the fact that Dr. Greene doesn't know that Carnegie made all his money in steel and iron, his estimate of Ivan is correct. The embryonic Dr. Schulze can tell you not only who wrote Tennyson's poems, but when the 1832 edition of the same author came out. And here you have him; an esoteric authority on things English, and, therefore (so say the C. E.'s), exoteric. Schulze came from City College, remained four short years among us, and now goes on, out and upward. Profoundly studious, he is one of that very low genus commonly appellated "Litterati." He is a keen and indefatigable student of literature, a clever essayist and a sound critic (remember Disraeli's definition of a critic). We all expect big things from Ivan, and confidently hope to see him some years hence an authority on the voluminous literature of the Ordovician.

name: Schuman, William
description: WILLIAM SCHUMAN, sole survivor of the esoteric and phthistic triumvirate more famous than the greatest of trios, "Wine, Woman and Song," graduated from Baltimore City College, matriculated with the best of us and defied the immortal gods by "doing" the pre-medical course in three years. "Willie" would be a doctor; yea, a modern Vesalius. He can tell you that monkey glands made Hugh Young; in what part of the head the thorax is; and why the Genus Amy appeals to the aesthetic in man. Moreover, "Willie" is an associate editor of this publication—which, in itself, is enough to damn him. He is inclined toward literature (this book?), and reads Mencken in the original, but is in no way a protagonist of the theories bruited abroad in Biology 2. Nevertheless and notwithstanding, Bill is a good student; yet a student who is neither a grind nor a pedant. His modesty, gentlemanliness and tact have made him a real comrade and friend of us all. In the medical field, Bill should be extremely successful. He has the talent, industriousness, and broad humanity that make a good medical man, yet tempers these qualities with the buoyancy of youth and the love of life which makes a man worth associating with. When "Willie" gives his associates that last handclasp in June he will leave behind with his teachers a profound respect for his talents and character, and will take with him the hopes and confidence of his friends. One Hundred and Forty-three

name: Murray Martin Seymour
description: MURRAY is rather choosy about the class with which he graduates. He has already let one class go by, and often expressed a doubt about permitting his name to be associated with the Class of '22. Seymour hails from the good old "Eastern Shore," and is what may very properly be termed a social engineer; that is, he hopes to achieve great things along this line—you know—marry the daughter of one of the directors or of the president of a great engineering company. This past summer he visited the Monkey Country (Brazil—to see some relatives?), and, according to his roommate, is now a sure cure for insomnia; the sufferer being entertained throughout the long hours of the night with tales of adventures which took place on the journey along the dots of the Equator. Murray M. is a lover of fiction; the modern kind, that comes wrapped in squares of vari-colored paper and requires weekly attention. If he had just uprooted himself and taken a more active part in college activities we do not doubt that he would have made an equally great name for himself—perhaps in adventures such as he now experiences in imagination alone. One Hundred and Forty-four

name: James Sylvan Shank
description: Well, folks, this is Jimmy Shank. It was decreed by fate that Jimmy should be the target for all jokes by the E. E.'s, and as he's had enough razzing there we'll spare him somewhat here. You see, Jimmy has not resigned himself to his fate, but comes back at us with every knock, so we really admire him for his spunk. Jimmy used to get us sore when he interrupted us in our work by putting his arms and body all over our papers, and started to firing blank questions at us in such rapid succession that we didn't have time to answer one before he had another one at us; and when we did have time to answer, he never bothered to listen to what we had to say, anyway. Yes, theory might have been a little difficult for Jimmy, but when it came to tracing out electrical connections, even a wiring system in Minos' Labyrinth could not confuse him. Jim's determination, indeed, has been an inspiration to us all, for the way some of us have "sat on" him has been something awful. We have sympathized with Jim on many such occasions, even when we ourselves were the inflictors of torture. Well, Jim, we'll bid you adieu here with best wishes, but don't let anybody bulldoze you, and don't be quite so ready to help. But let them know who you are, and show them that latent power within you.

name: Shank, Louis Warren
motto: Cursed be the man, the poorest wretch in life, The crouching vassal to the tyrant wife! —Burns.
description: The secret is out! Our friend is both a misogamist and misogynist. In fact, there is only one thing which he despises more than woman—and that is study. But Louis has the happy faculty of bluffing the profs into believing that he is a terrific worker—which, in itself, is work of a kind. Besides, he expects to reform when he reaches the Medical School. Yes, ferocious reader, Louis' ambition is to become a veterinarian, and to make an exhaustive research into the cause of epizootic and diabetes in the species taenia solium. He claims the veterinary to be the surgeon most in demand; profs are notably weak, and need constant treatment. Some twenty years hence we probably shall awake some morning to find that none other than our friend Louis has become world-renowned, married, and henpecked—though there is no necessary causal relation between the terms. But, all jocosity aside, Shank is an admirable fellow,—good-natured, a real friend, a hard worker (when he works), and, unless we are mistaken, he will some day become a successful physician—and a good husband.

name: Sharrets, Charles Roland
clubs: Varsity Baseball Team, 1, 2, 3, 4, and "H," 1, 2, 3, 4; Captain, 3; A. A. Board, 3, 4
description: Astronomers tell us that a year has four seasons, but "Shad" will admit the existence of only one—baseball season. Each spring he is one with the robins and squirrels in rendering a joyful paean of triumph to an all-generous nature. Once more the thud of ball against glove fills his soul with ecstasy. Once more the resounding crack of wood against leather is his symphony. Again he experiences the supreme thrill as he skillfully avoids base hits and screeching liners that make life in left field just one track meet after another. But, in addition to having hit better than .300 for four years on the Varsity, Shad has another less advertised accomplishment. That he is a geologist of parts, no one who has ever heard him perform with his little hammer will deny. So mean a hammer does he wield, and so expert is he in its use, that even the well-nigh impregnable "Faculty Bluff" has threatened to yield before his constant knocking. He is the only man who has ever made the race up Dan's Mountain interesting for Dr. Swartz. Shad has shown himself a master of the two professions to which he has devoted most of his time. If he decides to follow either of these, his success is certain; if he devotes his talents to hitherto untried fields, his all-around ability will stand him in good stead.

name: Shortess, George S.
description: SHORTESS is one of the few in the class who call work by its proper name—nearly the only one who is intimate enough to call it by its first name. Most of us have our dates and dances and frivolities and time-wasting pleasures, but Shortess seems to derive a peculiar satisfaction from working hard. He not only seems to enjoy it, but he actually waxes sleek and rotund on it, as the years pass by, and he continues to add to his vast store of knowledge. Perhaps it was during the period when he was a "Leatherneck" that he learned the value of time well spent; perhaps he didn't have to learn, but always knew. We don't know whether he likes the ladies or not; we can only present as evidence the fact that he has been holding down a part-time job in Mr. Ball's office, but we refuse to consider this evidence. One thing we do know, however; he is a shark in the Biology Lab, and that is a mighty bad place for sharks to hang out. We need not bother much about him; you can bet your last safety pin that he'll spread the fame of '22 before most of us get started.

name: Abraham Hamilton Silesky
motto: "It would talk; Lord, how it talked!" —Beaumont and Fletcher.
description: He is our champeen politician and string puller. We have never quite been able to discover his secret, but suspect his magic formula of success is diplomacy—for "Ham" is some diplomat (ask the "Sheik"). "Ham" came from Poly with the intention of becoming an engineer, but decided that it "pays to advertise," so he studies chemistry for recreation alone, while during off hours he is an active business man, specializing in multigraphs and advertising. Judging by his prosperous appearance, he must be highly successful. "Ham's" one weakness—or greatest strength—is the ladies. They find his benign physiognomy and raw loquacity irresistible. He goes the one-arm drivers one arm better—he has perfected a knee control for the steering wheel of his car, that is one of the marvels of modern science. His other virtues consist of a punk(tured) sense of humor, and an uncanny ability to evade work. All joking aside, "Ham" is a splendid fellow, and we predict a highly successful future for him in whatever line of activity he may choose for his life work.

name: Singewald, Quentin Dryer
description: Gentle reader, the photograph which perchance you mistook for the model of the Arrow Collar ads. is none other than that of our dear friend, Quentin Singewald. Quentin is Latin for fifth, so our Quentin is the fifth of his line; in other words, the fifth clothespin on the Singewald line is the sole possession of our worthy Quentin,—yea, even since his birth. So now we must write a paragraph on a single line, the fifth branch of the Singewald line. Speaking of lines, Quentin surely has an excellent one. By his line he persuaded some credulous business men to advertise in the "Jay," and as Circulation Manager of that worthy comic he circulated his line far and wide. Then he lined up the government to send him out in the wilds of Montana, where he spent a very restful summer, distinguishing a river from a sandy stream, a coyote from a groundhog. And it was by his line that in French he got nine. Quentin was known at college for his affability, his courtesy, his reserve, and his ability to bring pretty girls to Cotillions. Johnny Hopkins hates to see Quentin leave, but feels sure that he will uphold the reputation of the University wherever he may roam. So, on the side of the line, we take this time to bid Quentin a fond adieu.

name: Smith, Bernard Roddy
description: Here we have the young man who captivates all the girls by a mere glance. We learned first of this great power the day the Engineers went down the Bay to inspect the U. S. S. Maryland. Here he outshone everyone with his brilliant chatter and gray hat. "Smitty" also spends a good part of his time away from home, but he is usually on time in the mornings. (He lives just down the street.) Oral quizzes get his goat. "Smitty" will go into exile for a whole day before one of Dr. Whitehead's quizzes, and remember what Orsted discovered in 1820. Then Dr. Whitehead will ask him something entirely different and "Smitty" will tell him that it's no use talking when he doesn't know what to say. "Roddy's" favorite mark is 6.5, and he gives this in reply to all familiar questions. He also knows that force varies inversely as the square of the distance, hence his back seat during said quizzes. Still, "Smitty" is very proud of the fact that he is the best crammer in the University. "Roddy" was one of the E. E. mainstays who helped defeat the M. E.'s in baseball and football. His congenial Irish disposition (especially in regard to Schuchts) and his ability to make friends indicate a splendid future for "Roddy," even though he does spend considerable time in Walbrook.

name: Smith, Emil
description: EMIL is a much-married man and proud of it. Nor do we blame him, for his fate seems to have improved rather than marred the good disposition with which he was equipped when he entered college. Certain it is that Emil is rarely caught in a bad humor; in spite of the fact that he attends all the Mandolin Club rehearsals. Our friend is a musician of the first order, and during his stay at the University has been invaluable in his capacity of Director of the Band and Undergraduate Orchestra. Under his able leadership these activities have attained a recognized place on the Student Activities Council. The former has rendered active service in furnishing the nucleus of the rooting section, and the latter has proved its merit at the Dramatic Club productions and the Musical Clubs' concerts. Undaunted by the amount of work already upon his shoulders, when it was seen that the Mandolin Club needed a leader, Emil stepped into the gap and has filled it most satisfactorily. Emil is coming back next year to do graduate work, and we hope that he will find his way clear to continue his active participation in these activities, for in that event we need not worry for their future. Emil intends in time to be an adjuster of labor disputes; his success with the musicians out here means he will be a fortune if any employer in business can obtain his services.

name: Nicholas Maccubbin Smith
motto: "I am not lean enough to be thought a good student."
description: All those who have been in classes with Nic know what cheerful note his answer of "He-ere" added to the roll call. In fact, Nic is always ready to back his grin-provoking voice with a grin of his own, which might be said to come in the nick of time to many a student with a temporary attack of the blues. "He hath, indeed, a good outward happiness," as Bill Shakespeare was wont to say. Besides a cheerful voice and smile, his most prominent characteristics are his six feet and three inches, his Ford sedan, and his fondness for writing ten-page essays for Doc Mitchell. Of course, we did not mention his good looks—his photograph speaks for itself and them. You, tortured reader, are asked to give this picture more than a passing glance, for our biographical and prophetic researches have convinced us that it is the portrait of a future business man of no mean ability, taking "mean" to mean either average or "ornery." We wish him success in all he undertakes (though he does not expect to be an undertaker), and hope he will not let the name of Smith die out for want of progeny.
